text: A Critic’s Notebook article on Thursday about the film “Nosferatu” misstated the role of Renfield in the Bram Stoker novel “Dracula.” Renfield is Count Dracula’s servant, not Jonathan Harker’s employer.

An article on Wednesday about what The Times’s culture critics are looking forward to in 2025 attributed an erroneous distinction to the opera “Salome.” It was an early opera of Richard Strauss’s, not his first.

An article on Tuesday about Jimmy Carter’s lasting impact through his post-presidency work misstated the title of Dr. Helene D. Gayle. She is the former president of Spelman College, not the current one.

An article on Friday about the growing influence of Chinese companies and the Chinese government over shipping and global ports becoming a concern for U.S. officials misstated when President José Raúl Mulino came into office in Panama, and when the U.S. government put out an advisory cautioning about the security risks of foreign-made port equipment and software. Those events happened last year, not this year.

A Critic’s Notebook article on Thursday about the film “Nosferatu” referred incorrectly to the changes in the 1922 Murnau movie “Nosferatu” that were made to the character Van Helsing from Bram Stoker’s novel “Dracula.” Van Helsing had been reduced and remade into Professor Bulwer in the movie, not Professor Sievers. The article also described incorrectly the relationship of two characters in the novel. Quincey Morris was a suitor of Mina’s best friend, Lucy, not her fiancé.

An article this weekend on Page 3 about the history and impact of an American Dance Festival program to train dancers in China misidentified a person Yang Meiqi helped train. It was the dancer Duan Ni, who founded TAO Dance Theater with the choreographer Tao Ye, not Tao Ye.

An article this weekend on page 3 about the history and impact of an American Dance Festival program to train dancers in China misidentified a person Yang Meiqi helped train. It was the dancer Duan Ni, who founded TAO Dance Theater with the choreographer Tao Ye, not Tao Ye.

An article on Sunday about the recipients of the Presidential Medal of Freedom misstated which child of Robert F. Kennedy’s accepted the medal on his behalf. It was Kerry Kennedy, not Kathleen Kennedy.

An obituary on Sunday about the Swiss-born socialite Jocelyne Wildenstein misstated the location of the Versace mansion, where Lloyd Klein proposed to Ms. Wildenstein. It is in the city of Miami Beach, not Miami

Because of an editing error, an article on Jan. 3 about the recipients of the Presidential Citizens Medal misstated a distinction held by Joseph L. Galloway. He was the only civilian awarded a Bronze Star by the Army for combat valor in Vietnam, not the only civilian in the war to be awarded one.

An article this weekend on Page 14 about some of the music industry’s most respected and consistently employed roadies, instrument technicians and live sound people being well into their 60s and 70s misstated who recently retired, at 69. It was Lorne Wheaton, not Dallas Schoo. It also misstated the name of a musician. She is Steph Paynes, not Payne.

An article on Monday about a priest who turned a cathedral bell tower into a ham-curing workshop to raise funds for the restoration of the cathedral’s antique organ misstated the given name of the former rector of the cathedral. He is Philippe Boyer, not Gilles.

A picture caption with an article on Thursday about the wildfires spreading in and around Los Angeles misspelled the given name of a Pasadena police officer shown carrying Laverna Sharpe from her home in Altadena. He is Chrystian Banuelos, not Christian.

A Critic’s Notebook article on Saturday about cherished Los Angeles landmarks that have been damaged or destroyed by the deadly wildfires misstated the condition of the restaurant Gladstones. It has been partially damaged, but not burned down.

An article on Sunday about Susie Wiles, President-elect Donald J. Trump’s incoming White House chief of staff misstated the title of an official Elon Musk met with in November. The meeting was with Iran’s ambassador to the United Nations, not Iran’s ambassador to the United States.
An obituary on Saturday about Anita Bryant, the singer and former beauty queen, misstated the given name of Ms. Bryant’s mother. She was Lenora Bryant, not Lenore.

An article on Monday about Sid Edwards, the mayor of Baton Rouge, La., misstated which Louisiana cities elected white Republicans to succeed Black Democrats as mayors in their most recent elections. The cities are Baton Rouge and Shreveport, as the article noted — but not Alexandria, whose new mayor is a Democrat.

An article on Tuesday about Paul Manafort seeking business from political interests abroad misstated prosecutors’ characterization of an associate with whom Mr. Manafort discussed a plan to end hostilities between Ukraine and Russia. Prosecutors said the F.B.I. believed the associate had ties to Russian intelligence, not that the associate was a former Russian intelligence officer.

An article on Monday about the ways that undocumented immigrants help finance Social Security benefits misstated the status undocumented immigrants in the United States. There are 11 million undocumented immigrants, not 11 million undocumented workers.

An article on Wednesday about the House approving legislation that aims to bar transgender women and girls from participating in school athletic programs designated for female students misstated Representative Lori Trahan’s status as a former college athlete. She is the only former Division I female college athlete serving in Congress, not the only former Division I college athlete there.

An article on Wednesday about New York Gov. Kathy Hochul’s State of the State address on Tuesday described imprecisely a proposal by Ms. Hochul. It would protect the identities of doctors who prescribe drugs used in medication abortions, not of doctors who prescribe anti-abortion drugs.

An article on Wednesday about possible successors to Jamie Dimon at JPMorgan Chase misstated when Jennifer Piepszak would take over as chief operating officer at the bank. She is doing so immediately, not this summer.

Because of an editing error, an article on Wednesday about Saudi Arabia being close to acquiring up to a 10 percent stake in the sports-only network DAZN referred incorrectly to Gill Hind in one instance. She is Ms. Hind, not Mr. Hind.

Because of an editing error, an article on Jan. 8 about Elon Musk posting on his social media platform about a decade-old child sex abuse scandal in Britain described incorrectly one element of the government data on ethnicity and sexual abuse. The data referred to suspects in child sexual abuse cases in 2023, not to those convicted of a crime.

An article on Page 6 this weekend about 40 ideas to make New York City more affordable misspelled the name of the director of the Tri-State Transportation Campaign. She is Renae Reynolds, not Ranae Reynolds.

An article on Page 4 this weekend about the history of the Places to Go lists since 2005 refers imprecisely to statistics about the countries that have appeared on the list. The figures are through the 2024 list; they do not include the destinations on the 2025 list.
An obituary on Friday about the filmmaker David Lynch misstated when the television series “Twin Peaks,” which he created with Mark Frost, was seen on ABC. It was 1990 and 1991, not 1991 and 1992.

An article on Page 6 this weekend about 40 ideas to make New York City more affordable misspelled the name of the director of the Tri-State Transportation Campaign. She is Renae Reynolds, not Ranae Reynolds.

An article on Jan. 12 about the rise of Bucks County, Pa., as a celebrity hot spot misstated the location of the restaurant Canal House Station. It is in Milford, N.J., not Lambertville, N.J. The article also attributed an erroneous distinction to Canal House Station. It has not earned a Michelin star. And the article misstated the location of Ditto Vintage in New Hope, Pa. It is on Bridge Street, not Brick Street.
Because of an editing error, the On the Market feature on Dec. 15, 2024, misidentified a reporter who provided additional reporting. It was Jill P. Capuzzo, not Alicia Napierkowski.

An article on Page 4 this weekend about the history of the Places to Go lists since 2005 refers imprecisely to statistics about the countries that have appeared on the list. The figures are through the 2024 list; they do not include the destinations on the 2025 list.

An article on Sunday about polio survivors in the United States and their thoughts on the nomination of Robert F. Kennedy Jr. to be the nation’s next health secretary misspelled the given name of a polio survivor. She is Sunny Roller, not Sonny.

An obituary on Friday about the filmmaker David Lynch misidentified the state where Alvin Straight, the main character in Mr. Lynch’s movie “The Straight Story” (1999) lives. It is Iowa, not Wisconsin. (Wisconsin is the character’s destination, driving a John Deere lawn mower.)

An article on Friday about the hostage situation in Gaza misstated the name of the group that said two of the American hostages were believed to be alive. It is the American Jewish Committee, not American Jewish Council.
An obituary on Tuesday about Heinz Kluetmeier, a prominent photographer for Sports Illustrated, misspelled the given name of one of his daughters. She is Erika Kluetmeier, not Erica.

An article on Tuesday about the billionaires and multimillionaires who are flocking to Washington, D.C., to purchase luxury property misspelled the surname of President Joseph R. Biden Jr.’s White House chief of staff. It is Jeffrey Zients, not Zeints.

An article on Thursday about President Trump’s executive order to designate Mexican cartels and other criminal organizations as foreign terrorists and its potential impact on U.S. businesses misidentified the organization that conducted a survey of 218 companies. It was the American Chamber of Commerce in Mexico, not the U.S. Chamber of Commerce.

An article on Tuesday about JD Vance’s rise to the U.S. vice presidency misstated Mr. Vance’s age in relation to Richard M. Nixon’s when they were sworn in as vice president. Mr. Vance was four months older than Mr. Nixon, not four months younger, making Mr. Vance the third-youngest vice president.
A graphic on Jan. 18 about 2024 presidential vote margins by county mislabeled the key for the map. The blue circles indicated counties won by Kamala Harris, not Joseph R. Biden Jr.

An article on Wednesday about the signing of legislation that would allow a monument dedicated to American women to be built on the National Mall misspelled the given name of Franklin Delano Roosevelt’s labor secretary and the first woman to serve in a presidential cabinet. She was Frances Perkins, not Francis.

An article on Thursday about a new proposal to ease New York City’s housing crisis and make way for nearly 10,000 apartments in parts of Midtown Manhattan misquoted a portion of Councilman Erik Bottcher’s remarks on housing restrictions in Midtown Manhattan. He questioned limits on housing in “swaths of Midtown Manhattan,” not “slots of Midtown Manhattan.”

An article on Friday about bird flu infections in domestic cats misidentified the government agency that disclosed a potential link between the cat cases and turkey farms in Minnesota. The information came from a spokesman for the U.S.D.A., not the F.D.A.

An article on Friday about President Trump revoking security protection for Mike Pompeo, his former secretary of state, misstated the day that Mr. Trump was said to have revoked the security details. A person familiar with the matter said it was Wednesday, not Tuesday.
An article on page 6 this weekend misstates the duration of a drive Tom Green took from Ontario to Vancouver. The drive in total, without stops, may take as long as 50 hours, not 14.

An article on Page 28 this weekend about sugar addiction misstates the current edition of “Health Through Inner Body Cleansing: The Famous F. X. Mayr Intestinal Therapy From Europe.” It is the 44th edition, not the 43rd.
ref_headline: Health Through Inner Body Cleansing: The Famous F. X. Mayr Intestinal Therapy From Europe.

text: An article on Monday about the fragile cease-fires in Lebanon and Gaza referred incorrectly to the casualties caused by Israeli forces in southern Lebanon on Sunday. They killed or injured scores of people, according to Lebanese officials; they did not kill scores of people.

An article on Monday about President Trump’s phone call to Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen of Denmark, expressing his desire for the United States to take over Greenland misstated the year that Greenland ceased to be a colony of Denmark. It was 1953, not 1979.
An article on Saturday about the history of a gesture associated with the Nazis misstated the year Benito Mussolini came to power in Italy. It was 1922, not 1923.
An article on Monday about the novelist Scott Turow incorrectly characterized the defendant in the central murder case in “Presumed Guilty.” He is a college dropout, not a high school student.

An article on Tuesday about some oncologists recommending that the word “cancer” not be used to describe certain early cancers misstated the name of the organization at which Dr. Arif Kamal is chief patient officer. It is the American Cancer Society, not the American Cancer Association.

An article on Jan. 21 about an executive order signed by President Trump that withdrew the United States from the Paris Agreement climate agreement misstated the time frame for emissions cuts planned under President Biden. The goal was to cut greenhouse gases 61 percent below 2005 levels by 2035, not by 2030.
Because of an editing error, the item “Northbound Traffic” in the Metropolitan Diary column on Jan. 26 misstated when the events described occurred. It was about 20 years ago, not about 10.
An article on Page 40 this weekend about invasive crab species misstates the year Massachusetts awarded a grant to St. Ours & Co. It was 2023, not 2021.

Because of a production error, an article on Friday about the Trump administration disregarding the traditional checks and balances in its relationship with Congress, omitted the name of the No. 2 Republican in the Senate. He is Senator John Barrasso of Wyoming.

A picture caption with an article on Jan. 22 about Guatemala’s plans to resettle planeloads of deportees from the United States misidentified the state from which a deportation flight to Guatemala departed. It was Louisiana, not Virginia.

An article on Sunday about Secretary of State Marco Rubio’s first overseas trip misstated Michael E. Shifter’s role at the Inter-American Dialogue. He is a senior fellow, not the president (he stepped down from that role earlier).

An article on Saturday about former Vice President Mike Pence’s organization, Advancing American Freedom, running ads against Robert F. Kennedy Jr. for health secretary described imprecisely the Title X Family Planning Program. Federal law permits Title X clinics to make referrals to abortion providers, but only if the patient requests them. The clinics are not barred from doing so entirely.

Because of an editing error, an obituary on Sunday about the keyboardist Barry Goldberg referred imprecisely to what instruments Mr. Goldberg and Al Kooper played behind Bob Dylan at the 1965 Newport Folk Festival. Mr. Goldberg did play piano on two of the three songs, with Mr. Kooper on organ, but Mr. Kooper played bass and Mr. Goldberg played organ on the other song, “Like a Rolling Stone.” And a picture caption with the obituary misidentified one of the musicians taking part in a rehearsal with Mr. Dylan. He is Mr. Kooper, not Elvin Bishop.

An article on Wednesday about the Senate Finance Committee vote to send Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s nomination to the full Senate with a recommendation of approval misstated the office held by Senator Maria Cantwell’s father. He was a county commissioner, not a congressman.
An article on Jan. 26 about John Hodgman’s advice column misspelled the last name of the founder of the Maximum Fun podcast network. He is Jesse Thorn, not Thorne.

An article on Tuesday about Senator John Curtis, Republican of Utah, misattributed a social media post criticizing Senator Curtis and other Republicans. It was from an account impersonating Charlie Kirk, the conservative commentator and activist, not by Mr. Kirk.

An article on Wednesday about the lengths that research scientists in remote locations to follow their favorite teams gave the incorrect score of the Buffalo Bills game in a photo Meredith Nolan sent to her father. They were losing 21-10, not 27-10.

Because of an editing error, an obituary on Wednesday about the actress Merle Louise Simon referred incorrectly to Madame Armfeldt, the character she played in a production of “A Little Night Music.” It is Madame Armfeldt’s daughter, not Madame Armfeldt herself, who sings “Send in the Clowns.”

An obituary on Jan. 31 about the singer Marianne Faithfull misidentified the relative of Ms. Faithfull who invented a device he called the “Frigidity Machine.” It was Ms. Faithfull’s paternal grandfather, not her father.

Because of an editing error, an obituary about the Aga Khan IV, the leader of the world’s Ismaili Muslims, misstated a title given to Prince Rahim Al-Hussaini, the hereditary successor of the Aga Khan IV. He became the Aga Khan V, not the 50th. He is the 50th hereditary Imam, or spiritual leader, of the Shia Ismaili Muslims.

An article on Wednesday about President Trump’s desire to strike a deal with Ukraine for their rare earths and other minerals in exchange for American aid referred incorrectly to lithium, uranium and titanium as rare earth minerals. They are critical minerals, which differ from rare earth elements.

An article on Thursday about the C.I.A. sending an unclassified email listing all employees hired by the agency over the last two years to comply with an executive order from President Trump to shrink the federal work force misstated where the C.I.A. sent a list of employees. It went to the Office of Personnel Management, not to the Office of Management and Budget.

A picture caption with an article on Thursday about a lawsuit brought by Democrats in Nassau County, N.Y., to block the use of an armed volunteer force created by the county executive misidentified a lawmaker pictured at the lectern. He is Seth Koslow, a Democratic legislator, not Josh Kelner, a lawyer representing the lawmakers.

An article on Friday about efforts by the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform to learn about a project to screen elite Navy fighter pilots for signs of brain injuries caused by flying misstated the month of Capt. William Catlett’s death. It was in December 2024, not January 2025.

An article on Friday about a lawsuit that could potentially place the administrative state under the complete control of the president misidentified the party that had authority to remove the director of the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau for “inefficiency, neglect of duty or malfeasance in office.” It is the president, not Congress.
ref_headline: inefficiency, neglect of duty or malfeasance in office.

text: An article on Tuesday about the use of MDMA-assisted treatment for post-traumatic stress disorder misstated the year that Psymposia became a nonprofit. It registered as a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization in 2020, not when it was founded in 2014. The article also omitted Beatriz Labate’s affiliation with the Multidisciplinary Association for Psychedelic Studies, or MAPS. Dr. Labate works part-time for MAPS as a public education and culture specialist.
A Critic’s Notebook article on Friday about the rapper Kendrick Lamar misstated the place the album “DAMN” occupies in Mr. Lamar’s discography. It is his fourth studio album, not his third.

An article on Sunday about the State Department formally notifying Congress of its plans to move forward with sales of more than $8 billion in weapons to Israel misstated the day on which the Pentagon issued two news releases. It was Friday, not Thursday.

An obituary on Jan. 31 about the singer Marianne Faithfull misstated where she was when she overdosed on the barbiturate Tuinal in 1969. It was in a hotel room she shared with Mick Jagger in Australia, not on an airplane with him on the way there. In addition, the obituary misidentified the magazine to which Ms. Faithfull gave an interview in 2014. It was Vogue, not British Vogue. And it misstated her mother’s relationship to the author Leopold von Sacher-Masoch. She was his great-niece, not a descendant.

An obituary on Friday about the Metropolitan Opera singer Paul Plishka misstated the year that Mr. Plishka made his debut in the title role of “Boris Godunov,” at the Metropolitan Opera. It was 1983, not 1974.

Because of an editing error, an article on Sunday about an item from The Times’s Clippings Library misstated the job title of Louis Silverstein in 1962. He was then the art director for The Times’s promotion department, not the corporate art director.
An article on Monday about foreign leaders embracing flattery with President Trump misspelled the name of Japan’s prime minister. He is Shigeru Ishiba, not Ishida.

An article on Tuesday about the new tone of the Justice Department misidentified the prosecutor whom Attorney General Pam Bondi singled out by name in a memo. It was New York’s attorney general, Letitia James, not the district attorney of Fulton County, Ga., Fani T. Willis.
An article on Monday about what’s on television this week misstated the filming location for Season 8 of the reality show “Love Is Blind.” It is Minneapolis, not Ohio.

An article on Wednesday about the Ukrainian Parliament passing a contentious bill on Tuesday allowing the government to purchase two unused, Russian-made nuclear reactors from Bulgaria referred incorrectly to Bulgaria’s connection to the Soviet Union. It was a member of the Soviet bloc, but not part of the Soviet Union itself.
An article on Wednesday about the novelist Anne Tyler misspelled the surname of an author. She is Susan Muaddi Darraj, not Duraj.

An article on Page 20 this weekend about Charlie Kirk, the conservative activist and media personality, misstates the role of the Chinese government in the social platform TikTok. It is owned by ByteDance, a Chinese company subject to the government’s laws and regulations. The Chinese government does not own TikTok.

An article on Page 20 this weekend about Charlie Kirk, the conservative activist and media personality, misstates the role of the Chinese government in the social platform TikTok. It is owned by ByteDance, a Chinese company subject to the government’s laws and regulations. The Chinese government does not own TikTok.
An article on Sunday about the rise in thefts of popular weight-loss drugs in Brazil misstated the name of a company that owns a chain of pharmacies in the country. It is Grupo DPSP, not Grupo DPS.

An article on Monday about some of the country’s biggest liberal donors pausing their donations because of what they see as Democrats’ lack of vision and their concerns about retaliation from President Trump referred incorrectly to the Democracy Alliance’s internal practices. While the group is considering changing its document retention policies to hold digital communications for only a month, it has not yet made such a change.
An article on Saturday about couples getting married on Valentine’s Day misspelled the surname of the Bexar County, Texas, clerk. She is Lucy Adame-Clark, not Adama-Clark.

An article on Saturday about a federal judge’s move to extend an earlier judicial order that had temporarily restricted Elon Musk’s team’s access to the Treasury’s data and payment systems misidentified the lawyer who said that the two men designated as special government employees had received some training but that he was unsure what kind. It was Jeffrey Stuart Oestericher, a lawyer for the defense, not Andrew Stuart Amer, a lawyer for the plaintiffs.

An obituary on Saturday about former Gov. Jim Guy Tucker of Arkansas misspelled the surname of a stripper whose involvement with Mr. Tucker’s predecessor in the House of Representatives, Wilbur D. Mills, a former chairman of the House Ways and Means Committee, led to his forced retirement in the 1970s. She was Fanne Foxe, not Fox.
An article on Tuesday about the flood damage in Kentucky misstated the surname of a resident whose store was flooded in Whitesburg, Ky. She is Amanda Lucas, not Lewis.

An article on Tuesday about law enforcement officials finding no evidence that the torture and killing of a transgender man whose remains were found in a field in upstate New York last week was a hate crime misstated the surname of the victim. He was Sam Nordquist, not Norquist.

An article on Tuesday about a new book that focuses on the letters written by many Jews seeking refuge in the Netherlands who were denied entry after it closed its border in 1938 gave an incorrect date for the letter written by Jakob Aufrichtig. It was written in November 1938, not November 1939.

An article on Feb. 15 about Vice President JD Vance urging European leaders to end the isolation of far-right parties across the continent referred imprecisely to the reason behind Romania’s cancellation of its presidential election last year. While the election was canceled because of concerns about Russian manipulation and interference, there was not clear evidence that Moscow had done so in this case.

An article on Feb. 11 about the State Department issuing a stop-work order to all of the nonprofit organizations that it funds to end their demining work misstated an action taken by the U.N. agency responsible for monitoring global explosive contamination and funding action. A U.N. spokesman said the agency had not made a request to Secretary of State Marco Rubio for a waiver of the freeze.

An article on Feb. 15 about a Texas county seeking federal assistance to deal with contamination from toxic fertilizers described incorrectly efforts to test for PFAS on farms in Maine. While the authorities there have detected PFAS at dozens of dairy farms, they have closed five farms so far, not dozens.

An article on Sunday about the United Soccer League’s introduction of a new men’s professional league to rival that of Major League Soccer misstated the number of M.L.S. teams located in the United States. While it is the case that the league has 30 teams, only 27 of them are U.S.-based. The other three are based in Canada.

An article on Feb. 15 about a federal judge’s move to extend an earlier judicial order that had temporarily restricted access to Treasury Department data and payment systems by Elon Musk and his team misspelled the given name of the judge overseeing the hearing. She is Jeannette A. Vargas, not Jeanette.

An article on Thursday about what we know regarding the Trump administration’s transfer of more than 175 men from an immigration holding site in Texas to the U.S. military base at Guantánamo Bay misstated the number of prisoners awaiting trial at Guantánamo’s terrorism-related detention mission. It is six prisoners, not all 15 who remain.

A Critic’s Notebook article on Thursday about a film festival screening of the 1985 documentary “Shoah” misidentified the nationality of the directors and producers of a film. The film “Holding Liat” was directed and produced by Americans, not Israelis.
An article on Thursday about the Metropolitan Opera’s new season misspelled the surname of the director of “El Último Sueño de Frida y Diego.” She is Deborah Colker, not Kolker.
ref_headline: El Último Sueño de Frida y Diego.

text: An article on Thursday about the possible effect on the finances of the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts in Washington after President Trump purged the center’s board of all Biden appointees and installed himself as chairman described incorrectly the DeVos Institute of Arts Management. It is no longer affiliated with the University of Maryland.
An article this weekend on page 28 about the actor Jack Quaid misstates the surname of the actress who plays Mr. Quaid’s love interest on “The Boys.” It is Erin Moriarty, not Morgenstern.

An article this weekend on page 20 about George Clooney making his Broadway debut this spring misstates what Amal Clooney was doing when Mr. Clooney invited her to watch him score a movie. She was negotiating to protect women’s rights in Egypt’s new constitution, not working on a treaty with the Muslim Brotherhood.

An obituary on Thursday about the Mexican vocalist and songwriter Paquita la del Barrio misstated the name of the record label that gave the performer her first recording contract. It was Discos Musart, not CBS Records.
An article this weekend on Page 28 about the actor Jack Quaid misstates the surname of the actress who plays Mr. Quaid’s love interest on “The Boys.” It is Erin Moriarty, not Morgenstern.

An article this weekend on Page 20 about George Clooney making his Broadway debut this spring misstates what Amal Clooney was doing when Mr. Clooney invited her to watch him score a movie. She was negotiating to protect women’s rights in Egypt’s new constitution, not working on a treaty with the Muslim Brotherhood.

An article on Feb. 16 about the drug buprenorphine, an opioid that can help treat addiction to more serious opioids like heroin and fentanyl, misstated Kelly Pfeifer’s relationship with the California Health Care Foundation. She is not currently a director with the organization.

A chart on Tuesday with an article about Will Venable, the new manager of the Chicago White Sox, misstated the team’s winning percentage last season when it set a modern M.L.B. record with 121 losses. It was .253, not .248.

An article on Monday about what to watch on television this week omitted one of the creators of the new Netflix comedy “Running Point.” In addition to Ike Barinholtz, Mindy Kaling and Elaine Ko, who were mentioned in the article, David Stassen is a creator.

An article on Tuesday about a young woman who fled religious persecution in Iran and was then caught in the Trump administration’s deportation push misspelled the surname of an Iranian-American lawyer. He is Ali Herischi, not Herschi.

An article on Thursday about a New Jersey judge dismissing racketeering charges against George Norcross III misstated the position of Mr. Norcross’s lawyers on the claims outlined in the indictment. They accepted the facts as presented in the indictment to be true only for the purposes of a pretrial motion to dismiss the indictment; not that the claims were accurate but did not constitute a crime.

An article on Thursday about a poverty tracker report for New York City misidentified the research group that analyzed Gov. Kathy Hochul’s proposals to reduce child poverty. It was the Urban Institute, not the Center on Poverty and Social Policy at Columbia University. The article also misstated the estimated reduction in child poverty from these proposals. It is about 18 percent for New York State, not 17 percent for New York City.

An article on Friday about President Trump’s proposal for a new “gold card” visa program described incorrectly a golden visa program in the European Union. Some golden visa holders are entitled to visa-free travel within certain areas of the European Union, but residence is restricted to the specific country that issued the visa; it is not the case that they allow recipients to establish residency in any country in the bloc.
An article on Friday about the Netflix comedy “Running Point” omitted one of the creators of the show. Elaine Ko was a creator along with Ike Barinholtz, Mindy Kaling and David Stassen.

An article on Page 22 this weekend about Parker Posey misspells the surname of an actress. She is Michelle Monaghan, not Moynahan. And the article misspelled the surname of a filmmaker. He is Greg Mottola, not Motolla.
An obituary on Friday about the actor Gene Hackman misstated the age of his wife, Betsy Arakawa. She was 65, not 64.

An article on Page 22 this weekend about Parker Posey misspells the surname of an actress. She is Michelle Monaghan, not Moynahan. And the article misspelled the surname of a filmmaker. He is Greg Mottola, not Motolla.
An obituary on Friday about the actor Gene Hackman misstated the name of the 2001 movie in which he played a tobacco tycoon. It is “Heartbreakers,” not “The Heartbreakers.”

An article on Saturday about China flexing its military muscle in the Pacific paraphrased incorrectly Adm. Samuel J. Paparo’s position on U.S. preparations for China’s military buildup. He said the United States is increasingly concerned about the Chinese buildup, and he called for greater urgency and efficiency in the U.S. response, not that U.S. forces were ill-prepared for China’s military buildup in the Asia-Pacific.

An article on Sunday about migration from the United States to Canada misstated the number of irregular migrants apprehended at the U.S. borders. At the northern border, nearly 24,000 migrants were apprehended in 2024, not 200,000. In January 2024, it was about 900, not 15,000. And in January this year, it was about 600, not 5,000. At the southern border, over 1.5 million were apprehended last year, not two million. The higher numbers counted everyone stopped by U.S. authorities while crossing the border, but the correct figures, which are far lower, include only those who were found to be crossing illegally, according to data published by the U.S. Customs and Border Protection agency.
An article on Saturday about Canadians’ protests of the Trump administration’s economic policies misspelled the surname of the “Late Night” host. He is Seth Meyers, not Myers.

An article on Saturday about the Chinese electronics maker Xiaomi incorrectly described the factory where Xiaomi’s SU7 electric car was produced. It was manufactured in a Xiaomi facility, not one taken over from the Beijing Auto Group. The article also paraphrased incorrectly comments about the SU7 by Xiaomi’s chief executive, Lei Jun. He said his goal was that the SU7 performs like a Porsche, not that it looks like one.

Because of an editing error, a picture caption with an article on Friday about what to do in New York City over the weekend contained inaccurate information about the festival Crankies Take New York! at Flushing Town Hall. The company Alex and Olmsted, who are shown in the photograph, did not appear at the festival.

An article on Saturday about an upcoming Sotheby’s auction misstated the year Venice landscapes by the Rococo-era painter Francesco Guardi were bought at auction for $5.6 million. It was 2000, not 2020.

Because of an editing error, an article on Monday about an adaptation of “Moby Dick” opening at the Metropolitan Opera this week misstated where the opera had its premiere. It was at the Dallas Opera, not in Houston.

An obituary on Sunday about the singer and songwriter David Johansen referred incorrectly to a fund-raising campaign to help pay Mr. Johansen’s medical bills. It was through the Sweet Relief Musicians Fund, not GoFundMe.

An article on Tuesday about efforts by President Claudia Sheinbaum of Mexico to hold U.S. gun manufacturers liable for gun violence in Mexico misstated the timing of President Trump’s announcement that he was delaying tariffs on Mexico. It was last month, not this month.

An article on Tuesday about the Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company committing to spend $100 billion in the United States over the next four years to expand its production capacity misstated the number of manufacturing plants that TSMC would operate in Arizona after its planned expansion there. It is six, not five.

An obituary on Feb. 2 about the photographer George Tice omitted the name of one of the directors of the documentary “George Tice: Seeing Beyond the Moment.” It was directed by Bruce Wodder and Peter Bosco, not just by Mr. Wodder.
ref_headline: George Tice: Seeing Beyond the Moment.

text: An article on Feb. 25 about the ways Germany’s hard-right AfD party finds most of its support from citizens in former East Germany referred imprecisely to the controlled political parties of East Germany. The East German communist system included separate parties of nominal Christian Democrats and Liberal Democrats, but not, for most of its existence, of nominal Social Democrats.

An article on Feb. 28 about pro-Russian politicians in Ukraine using social media posts to criticize President Volodymyr Zelensky and echo Russia’s call for elections in Ukraine referred incorrectly to Oleksandr Dubinsky’s status. He is still a member of the Ukrainian Parliament, and he is in jail awaiting trial on a charge of treason. He is not a former member of Parliament, and he has not been convicted or sentenced to prison. The errors were repeated in a picture caption.

An article on Wednesday about the harm that retaliatory tariffs by China, Canada and Mexico could cause to American farmers misstated the amount of American goods that were facing immediate tariffs in Canada. It is $20.5 billion, not $30 billion.
An obituary on Wednesday about the landscape designer M. Paul Friedberg misstated the year that The New York Times published an article on adventure playgrounds. It was 1999, not 1995.
An article on Thursday about new details in the killing of a 24-year-old transgender man, Sam Nordquist, misstated Thomas Eaves’s age. He is 21, not 19.
An article on Page 22 this weekend about the film director Bong Joon Ho misstated the number of feature films Bong Joon Ho has made. “Mickey 17” is his eighth, not his seventh.

A picture caption with an article on Page 92 this weekend misidentifies a model on one of the issue’s two covers; he is Santino Guzman at Vision Los Angeles, not Roman Leal at State Management. The mistake was repeated with an image in the issue’s table of contents. Two picture captions with the article misspell the surname of a model; he is Jonathan Gonzalez, not Gonzales. Another picture caption with the article lists the incorrect agencies for three models: Shea Estrin, Caleb Enochs and Santino Guzman were all booked through Vision Los Angeles, not through LA Models, State Management and Soul Artist Management, respectively.
Because of an editing error, an article on Page 88 about contemporary Finnish saunas refers incorrectly to the curator Saija Silen. She is a woman.

An article on Sunday about the 41 hostages who have been killed since being taken captive by Hamas and its allies during their Oct. 7 attack on Israel misstated when Israel’s military concluded that an airstrike had killed hostages. It was in March 2024, not March 2023.

An article on Saturday about a South Korean court ordering the release from jail of the country’s impeached president Yoon Suk Yeol misstated the timing of the indictment. It was in January, not last month.

An article on Sunday about the introduction of price controls by Croatia’s government to rein in inflation misidentified the name of the research institute where John H. Cochrane works. It is the Hoover Institution, not the Hoover Institute.

An article on Saturday about the robotic Athena moon lander spacecraft’s inability to charge its batteries after ending up on its side misstated how far the craft was from its targeted landing site. It was about 800 feet, not 150 meters.

A column by The Athletic on Sunday about whether Pete Rose should be in the Baseball Hall of Fame referred incorrectly to his World Series history. Rose played on championship teams for the Cincinnati Reds in 1975 and 1976 and for the Philadelphia Phillies in 1980; he did not play on three World Series winners during the days of Cincinnati’s Big Red Machine.

An article on Monday about the video game designer Josef Fares misspelled the given name of a video game composer. He is Gustaf Grefberg, not Gustav. It also erroneously included a game in his credits; he did not write the score for Wolfenstein: The New Order.

An article on Sunday about the impact that President Trump and his policies are having on the primary races for New Jersey governor misidentified the branch of the military in which Representative Mikie Sherrill served. She was a helicopter pilot in the Navy, not the Army.
An article on Tuesday about the legacy of Covid-19 misstated the number of American deaths in the last week of 2021. It was 10,476, not 463,267.
An article on Tuesday about identifying when pain should be considered a red flag in a workout misstated the amount a healthy knee should bend. It is around 130 to 150 degrees, not 160 degrees.

An article on Wednesday about Israel and Lebanon agreeing to start negotiations on disputes over their land border referred incorrectly to a statement from the Israeli prime minister’s office. It said that Israel had agreed to release five Lebanese detainees, not that it had released them.

A headline with an article on Tuesday about Mark Carney, the head of the Liberal Party who will soon lead Canada, referred incorrectly to his new title. As the head of Canada, he will be the prime minister, not the premier, a distinction that is reserved for leaders of the nation’s provinces or territories.
An article on Wednesday about job cuts at NASA’s Washington headquarters misstated the surname of a NASA spokeswoman. She is Cheryl Warner, not Wheeler.

A news analysis article on Thursday about shifting political winds in the Philippines misstated the day when President Ferdinand R. Marcos Jr. received the arrest warrant for his predecessor, Rodrigo Duterte. It was on Tuesday, not Monday.

An article on Thursday about the impact that cuts to the Department of Education’s research employees and outside partners will have on research and data collection misstated the amount of money that Mathematica won in federal funding last year. It was $360 million, not $1.3 billion.

An article on Thursday about the different limits on gun permits for people convicted of minor charges and those convicted of domestic violence charges misstated the year that a provision barring people subject to a qualifying domestic violence protection order from possessing guns was passed. It was 1994, not 1984.

An article on Thursday about the Environmental Protection Agency reframing the purpose of the agency’s mission referred imprecisely to the views of the Climate Enterprise Institute. The group believes global warming has been minimal and good for humanity and the biosphere; it does not promote “climate denial.”

An article on Monday about Israel’s decision to cut off electricity to the Gaza Strip, relying on information from a source at the Israeli electric company, misstated the type of service affected by the electricity shut-off in Gaza. It was a desalination plant, not a wastewater treatment plant.

An article on Thursday about the impact that cuts to the Department of Education’s research employees and outside partners will have on research and data collection misstated the amount of money that A.I.R. had in federal funding last year. It was $236 million, not $600 million.
An article on Friday about a lawsuit challenging the Trump administration’s firing of more than 1,300 workers at the Department of Education incorrectly described Pell grants. They are not loans.

An article on Monday about Mahmoud Khalil, a former graduate student at Columbia University who participated in student protests against the war in Gaza, misstated Mr. Khalil’s status in the United States. He is a permanent resident, not a permanent citizen.

Because of an editing error, an article on Saturday about Makoto Watanabe, the co-founder and editor in chief of the Tokyo Investigative Newsroom Tansa, misstated when Tansa published a series of articles that exposed forced sterilizations. The articles were published from 2018 to 2021, not last year.

An article on Monday about firms that are hiring government relations professionals with ties and connections to President Trump to help them navigate shifting diplomatic alliances, unexpected conflicts and an unpredictable American administration misspelled the target of the raid that William McRaven oversaw. The target was Osama bin Laden, not Osama bin Ladin.

An article on Sunday about Netflix’s adaptation of internationally significant novels into streamed series omitted an element of Gabriel García Márquez’s name in subsequent references throughout the article. He was García Márquez, not simply Márquez.

A picture caption with an article on Saturday about Robert Indiana misstated the closing date of an exhibition. It closes March 29; it does not open on March 29. Another picture caption with the article also misidentified the gallery exhibiting an artwork. “The American Dream,” from 1992, is in the Pace exhibition, not the Kasmin exhibition.

A picture caption with an article on Monday about the Broadway play “Othello” incorrectly described what fans were doing outside the Ethel Barrymore Theater. They were waiting to see Denzel Washington after the performance, not waiting to get into the show.

A news analysis article on Wednesday about Israel’s resumed attacks in Gaza after stalled cease-fire talks with Hamas referred incorrectly to statements made by Hamas after the Israeli strikes. The group called on Palestinians to step up attacks in the West Bank, but it did not vow an immediate response from Gaza in any formal statements.

An obituary on Tuesday about Dr. Sheldon Greenfield, who was known for his research into gaps in health care, misstated the distance between Venice, Calif., where he lived in the 1970s and ’80s, and Torrance, where he once had to drive his family’s babysitter to a public hospital. It is about 20 miles, not almost 40.

An article on Thursday about Canada’s deal with Australia to develop a cutting-edge radar for the Arctic that can detect hypersonic missiles and other threats over the curvature of the earth misstated when the joint U.S.-Canada aerospace command was established. It was established in the 1950s, not the 1980s.

An article on Friday about beef tallow misstated the nature of a $1.5 million payment received by the American Heart Association. It was a donation from listeners of a radio program sponsored by Procter & Gamble, not a corporate sponsorship from Procter & Gamble itself.

A picture caption with an article on Tuesday about Lucian Simmons, the new head of provenance research at the Metropolitan Museum of Art, misstated the era of a bronze griffin being returned to Greece. It is from the seventh century B.C., not the seventh century.

A picture caption with an article on Friday about the Frick Museum reversed the identities of the painter and subject in a portrait. The image was Sir Anthony van Dyck’s portrait of Frans Snyders, not Snyder’s portrait of van Dyck.
An article on Page 8 this weekend about the actress Ellen Pompeo misstates the type of work that her husband, Chris Ivery, does. Ivery works in marketing; he is no longer a music producer.

An article on Page 30 this weekend about solidarity transcribes incorrectly a line from Sarah Schulman’s forthcoming book, “The Fantasy and Necessity of Solidarity.” The line is “Solidarity is the action behind the revelation that each of us, individually, are not the only people with dreams,” not “Solidarity is the action behind the revelation that each of us, individually, are the only people with dreams.”
ref_headline: The Fantasy and Necessity of Solidarity.

text: An article on Saturday about the closure of Heathrow Airport in London after a fire at an electrical substation misidentified the kind of equipment that Heathrow’s chief executive said had worked as expected on Friday. It was backup generators, not a backup transformer.

Because of an editing error, an article on Saturday about Columbia University’s agreement to overhaul its protest policies, security practices and Middle Eastern studies department referred incorrectly to the Center for Palestine Studies and the Institute for Israel and Jewish Studies at the university. They are distinct from the Middle Eastern studies department, not part of it.

An article on Saturday about an event at which President Trump signed an executive order to diminish the Department of Education misidentified who was among a group of Republican governors who attended the signing ceremony. Gov. Glenn Youngkin of Virginia was not in attendance.

An obituary on Monday about Max Frankel, a former executive editor of The New York Times, referred incorrectly to the impact of the 1987 stock market crash. It led to an advertising slump in the newspaper industry, not a national recession. The obituary also misstated the position Richard C. Holbrooke held at the United Nations. He was the U.S. ambassador to that body, not a delegate to it.

An article on March 18 about exemptions from military service in Israel misstated the extent of military exemptions for ultra-Orthodox men. From 1948 to 1977, annual exemptions were capped, meaning some, not all, ultra-Orthodox men were exempt from mandatory military service.

An article on Tuesday about a vote that could secure child care vouchers for low-income families in New York City misstated the surname of the chief policy and innovation officer of the Day Care Council of New York. He is Gregory Brender, not Bender.

An article on Saturday about the artwork of the novelist Flannery O’Connor, based on information from Georgia College & State University, misstated Louise Florencourt’s age when she died. She was 97, not 99.

An article on Saturday about the Japanese musician and visual artist Hiroshi Yoshimura misstated which album Brian Eno made after an automobile accident. It was “Discreet Music,” not “Ambient 1: Music for Airports.”

An article on March 18 about the growth of an online DIY subculture focused on quitting psychiatric medications misidentified Dr. Mark Horowitz, the co-author of the Maudsley Deprescribing Guidelines. He is a psychiatric resident, not a psychiatrist.

An article on Sunday about archival photographs of the Dutch-born artist Willem de Kooning referred incorrectly to de Kooning’s time in East Hampton, N.Y., in 1951. Though de Kooning spent time in East Hampton in the early 1950s, he did not yet own a property there.

An article on Thursday about the U.S.-operated military base in northwestern Greenland referred imprecisely to the New York Air National Guard’s summer operations. During that period, the contingent primarily operates out of Kangerlussuaq Airport, not out of Pituffik.

Because of an editing error, an article on Monday about the scuttling of a plan to open a long-awaited pedestrian path on the Queensboro Bridge referred incorrectly to the roadway from which the bridge’s existing walking and bike path was carved. It is the bridge’s northern outer roadway, not the outer northbound roadway.

A picture caption with an article on Thursday about Elon Musk’s support for a conservative candidate for the Wisconsin Supreme Court while Tesla is suing the state over a law restricting direct-to-consumer vehicle sales referred imprecisely to Mr. Musk’s relationship to Tesla. While he co-founded and owns the largest individual stake in the company, he does not own it outright.

An article on Thursday about the Norwegian black metal band Mayhem celebrating its 40th anniversary misstated Per Ohlin’s role in Mayhem. He was not the band’s first singer, there were several vocalists before him.

An article on Monday about a rediscovered painting of an African prince by Gustav Klimt that is under negotiation for sale misstated the position of the estate stamp on Klimt’s painting. It was on the front, not the back.

An article on Tuesday about a tour led by an 88-year-old guide in Bastogne, Belgium, the scene of a critical battle of World War II, referred imprecisely to the German’s military successes there in late 1944 and early 1945. While they recaptured the area surrounding Bastogne from U.S. forces, they did not recapture the town itself. The Americans held the town and then freed the entire area during the Battle of the Bulge.
An article on Page 9 about the short film “The Ballad of Wallis Island” misstates the film’s wide release date. It is April 18, not April 11.
An article last weekend about the actress Ellen Pompeo’s role in a new Hulu series misstated her husband Chris Ivery’s line of work. Ivery works in marketing; he is no longer a music producer.

An article on Page 7 this weekend about “Severance” includes an inaccurate plot detail involving Miss Huang, a character on the show. She turns in another character to management for his use of paper clips, not staples.

An article last weekend about Germany’s struggle to handle the physical remains of its Nazi past misstated the year that Tulle, France, was attacked by resistance forces. It was June 7, 1944, not 1945.

An essay last weekend about the prospects for progressive opposition in the second Trump era transcribed incorrectly a line from Sarah Schulman’s forthcoming book, “The Fantasy and Necessity of Solidarity.” The line is “Solidarity is the action behind the revelation that each of us, individually, are not the only people with dreams,” not “Solidarity is the action behind the revelation that each of us, individually, are the only people with dreams.”
ref_headline: The Fantasy and Necessity of Solidarity.

text: A picture caption with the table of contents on Page 28 misidentifies the designer of a pair of white pants. They are the stylist’s own, not from Celine by Hedi Slimane.
An article about Black satire on Page 82 refers incorrectly to the writer Danzy Senna’s father, Carl Senna. Mr. Senna is not deceased.
