(Close to 1,000 entries on the spreadsheet appear to be duplicates. And some entries appear to represent Kazakhs, not Uyghurs.)

An article this weekend on Page 12 about six hidden shops in New York misstates the profession of Daniel Corral-Webb, a co-founder of Archivio Records. He is a D.J., not a designer. The article also misstates the online presence of High Valley Books. The shop does have a website.

An article on Saturday about the attacks in Israel and Iran on Friday misstated which Iranian official addressed the United Nations Security Council on Friday. It was Iran’s U.N. ambassador, Amir Saeid Iravani, not Iran’s foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi.

An article on Saturday about the victims of the Air India flight from Ahmedabad to London’s Gatwick Airport that crashed into the facilities of a nearby medical college misstated the surname of a family friend of one of the crash victims. He is Sanjay Jhajjar, not Chhibber.

An article on Saturday about the Trump administration’s shifting rationale for detaining Mahmoud Khalil after a federal judge ruled that the government could not continue to hold the Columbia University graduate under its initial justification misspelled the surname of a former homeland security official. He is Scott Shuchart, not Schuchart.
An article on Monday about the wedding of Huma Abedin and Alex Soros’s wedding in the Hamptons misstated Ms. Abedin’s age. She is 48, not 49.

An article on Friday about the six-episode Australian murder mini-series “The Survivors” misidentified the character Mia in the series. Mia is Kieran’s girlfriend and the mother of his child; she is not his wife.

An obituary on June 11 about Frederick Forsyth, the author of “The Day of the Jackal” and other thrillers, referred incorrectly to an incident in 1973 when Mr. Forsyth, working for British intelligence, traveled to Dresden, which was then in East Germany. Although he recalled that Vladimir V. Putin was the K.G.B. station chief there at the time, Mr. Putin did not join the K.G.B. until 1975 and was not stationed in Dresden until 1985.

An obituary on Sunday about Harris Yulin, the award-winning actor and director, misstated part of the name of a play in which Mr. Yulin appeared in 1963. It is “Next Time I’ll Sing to You,” not “Next Time I’ll Sing for You.”
ref_headline: Next Time I’ll Sing to You,

text: A video game review on Saturday about the role-playing adventure game Deltarune misidentified one of the character’s childhood friends. She is Noelle Holiday, not Carol.

A picture caption with an article on Thursday about President Trump’s shifted stance toward Iran under pressure from Israel misidentified which replicated Jerusalem building people in Iran were gathered around. The building is modeled after the Dome of the Rock, not Al Aqsa Mosque.

A picture caption with an article on Friday about an American artist who left the United States for Kyiv and was one of at least 28 people killed in a large-scale Russian drone and missile attack misspelled the surname of the individual. He was Fred Grandy, not Grady.

An article on June 10 about the use of the powerful pesticide paraquat in suicides in Suriname referred imprecisely to the sale of paraquat in that country. While the pesticide was developed by the chemical company Syngenta in the 1960s, Syngenta does not sell or market paraquat in Suriname.

An article on June 18 about Maj. Erica Vandal’s dismissal from the military because of President Trump’s transgender ban misstated the type of gun salute her father, Lt. Gen. Thomas Vandal, received at his funeral. It was a 15-gun salute, not a 21-gun salute.

An article on Saturday about Mahmoud Khalil, a pro-Palestinian campus protester detained by the Trump administration, being released on bail Friday misspelled the given name of a lawyer arguing on behalf of the government. He is Dhruman Sampat, not Drhuman Sampat.
An article on Sunday about New York City’s mayoral primary misstated the duration of early voting in the 2021 mayoral primary. It ran for nine days, not 10.

An article on June 1 about the Trump administration terminating a $258 million program to work on H.I.V. vaccines misspelled the name of a county in North Carolina. It is Mecklenburg, not Mecklenberg.

An article on Friday about some of the most noteworthy legislation that passed in June by New York State’s Legislature misspelled the surname of the state senator who cosponsored a bill meant to pre-empt any potential rollback of housing discrimination laws. He is Brian Kavanagh, not Kavanaugh.

An article on Saturday about Adrienne Adams rejecting pressure to cross-endorse Zohran Mamdani for New York City mayor misstated the month that Mr. Mamdani made his first campaign appearance at a Black church. His first such visit was in February, not April.

An article on Sunday about an experimental treatment that involved a single infusion of a stem cell-based treatment, which may have cured 10 out of 12 people with the most severe form of type 1 diabetes, misstated where new islet cells that were infused took up residence. It was in the liver, not the pancreas

An article on Monday about Mahmoud Khalil giving his first interview since his release from detention on Friday misspelled the given name of the author of “Man’s Search for Meaning.” He was Viktor Frankl, not Victor.

Because of an editing error, a picture caption with an article on June 18 about the claim of Herkimer, N.Y., that it, and not Springfield, Mass., is the actual birthplace of basketball misstated the given name of the game’s acknowledged inventor. He was Dr. James Naismith, not John.
An article on June 13 about 30 television shows to watch this summer misstated the premiere date of the Apple TV+ series “Stillwater.” It is Aug. 1, not Aug. 4.

An article on June 19 about the challenges facing Art Basel’s flagship event in Switzerland misstated the planned size of Igor Lah’s private museum in Slovenia. It will be more than 50,000 square feet, not 25,000.

An article on Tuesday, using information provided by a source about some of the discoveries that the Vera C. Rubin Observatory in Chile is expected to make, misstated how close an asteroid seen by Rubin will pass by Earth. It is 70,000 miles, not 60,000.

An article on Wednesday about South Korea’s concerns that China could use its large steel cages and a former oil drilling rig in the Yellow Sea to expand its military influence misspelled the name of one of the steel cages. It is the Shen Lan 2, not the Shan Lan 2.

An article on Thursday about Robert F. Kennedy’s decision to withdraw U.S. financial support from the leading international vaccine organization, Gavi, misstated how often a summit is held to replenish its finances. The summit is held every five years, not every four years.

Because of an editing error, an article on June 21 about a French lawmaker’s efforts to reverse a decision to deny him entry to the United States referred imprecisely to the Electronic System for Travel Authorization program. It applies to international visitors to the United States from countries with certain visa waiver arrangements, not to all international visitors.

An obituary on Tuesday about the architect Nathan Silver, whose 1967 book, “Lost New York,” discussed buildings that had been demolished before the city passed a landmarks preservation law, referred incorrectly to the Singer Tower in Manhattan. It was not included in that edition; although it was later demolished and was included in the 2000 edition, it was still standing at the time.

An article on Friday about the impact that Republican efforts to reduce federal funding flowing to schools has had on Johns Hopkins University misstated the source of a Trump administration plan to cut about 40 percent of the budget for the National Institutes of Health. It is contained in a budget proposal, not in the Trump policy bill under consideration in Congress.

An article on Thursday about U.S. rivals celebrating the Trump administration’s decision to cut its support for Voice of America and Radio Free stations referred incompletely to the number of followers on Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty’s Instagram accounts. It has 44,500 followers on its English-language account and a total of 17.4 million followers across the network’s various language services. It does not have a total of 44,500 followers.
An article this weekend on Page 12 about the Brazilian artist Luana Vitra includes outdated information. Hélio Menezes is no longer the director of the Museu Afro Brasil in São Paulo.
An article this weekend on Page 12 about the Brazilian artist Luana Vitra includes outdated information. Hélio Menezes is no longer the director of the Museu Afro Brasil in São Paulo.

An article on Saturday about Zohran Mamdani’s campaign promises while running for mayor of New York City referred incorrectly to the New York City Council. It is not the case that the Council has never passed a minimum wage law. It did so in 1964, but the law was struck down in court.

An article on Saturday about Susan Namangale and her efforts to introduce the game of chess in Malawi, misstated the size of the family into which Ms. Namangale was born. It was a family of eight, not seven.

An article on Sunday about the four police officers in France who were ordered to stand trial on charges that they assaulted a Black man in November 2020 misspelled the name of the fourth police officer identified as a defendant by prosecutors. The officer identified by prosecutors is Hugues R., not Hughes R.

An article on Sunday about the rise in political violence in the United States misstated the events that followed Thomas Crooks’s assassination attempt on Donald Trump at a Butler, Pa., campaign event. Mr. Crooks was not charged; he was killed by the Secret Service that day.

Because of an editing error, an article on Monday about Republican Senator Thom Tillis’s announcement that he will not seek re-election in North Carolina next year misstated the political affiliation of Andy Nilsson, a candidate for the U.S. Senate from North Carolina. Mr. Nilsson is a Republican, not a Democrat.
An article on Tuesday about a heat wave sweeping much of Europe incorrectly described Puerta del Sol. It is one of the largest squares in Madrid, not the largest.

An article on Saturday about the Supreme Court’s decision to uphold a provision of the Affordable Care Act that requires insurance companies to offer some kinds of preventive care for free misstated the requirement of the Affordable Care Act’s individual mandate. The mandate requires that people obtain health insurance or pay a fine, not that most employers provide health insurance for their workers.
An obituary on Tuesday about the former congressman Pat Williams misstated the number of grandchildren who survive him. It is three, not five.

An article on Wednesday about the Senate’s vote-a-rama on President Trump’s domestic policy bill, using information from Senate officials, referred incorrectly to the vote-a-rama session. It came close to breaking a record for the most recorded votes cast during such a session; it did not break that record.

An article on Wednesday about the sentencing of a man who killed a paramedic in a random attack in 2022 referred incorrectly to Alison Russo at the time she was attacked. She was walking near her station in Astoria, Queens. She was not walking back to it.
An article on Wednesday about the singer and songwriter Neil Sedaka misstated the release date of his last major compositional effort, “Manhattan Intermezzo.” It was 2012, not 2016.

A headline with an article on Wednesday about an explosion at a pharmaceutical factory in the southern Indian state of Telangana misstated the number of people killed by the blast. As the article correctly noted it was 36, not 26.

Because of an editing error, a picture caption with an article on Thursday about a federal judge’s ruling that the Trump administration cannot categorically deny asylum claims from people crossing the southern border referred incorrectly to immigrant processing in Tapachula, Mexico. The city is in the south of Mexico, not near a U.S. port of entry.

An article on Page 38 this weekend about Luke Littler, widely considered to be the world’s best darts player, misstates the duration of the Premier League darts competition. It is four months long, not six.

An article on Wednesday about four friends who took a weekend trip to Texas’s Hill Country before the catastrophic flooding began misstated Aidan Heartfield’s age. Mr. Heartfield, who remains missing, is 22, not 21.
An article on Wednesday about notable new songs of the week misstated the release month for Trio of Bloom’s upcoming album. It is September, not November.
An obituary on Sunday about the entrepreneur S. Daniel Abraham misstated when his marriage to Estanne Weiner ended in divorce. It was 1985, not 1993.

An article on Wednesday about the Oloffson Hotel in Haiti being burned to the ground last Saturday misstated the nationality of the family that took over the hotel. The family, the Oloffsons, were German-Haitian, not Norwegian-Haitian.
An article on July 5 about the ways that Yemeni cafes are building momentum and community misspelled the first name of a commercial real estate investor. He is Don Tepman, not Dan.

An article this weekend on Page 6 about New Jersey Transit’s locomotive 4207, the oldest in regular passenger service in the entire country, misstated the name of the NJ Transit maintenance site. It is the Meadows Maintenance Complex, not the Meadowlands Maintenance Complex.

An article this weekend on Page 6 about New Jersey Transit’s locomotive 4207, the oldest in regular passenger service in the entire country, misstated the name of the N.J. Transit maintenance site. It is the Meadows Maintenance Complex, not the Meadowlands Maintenance Complex.

An article on Monday about how deep-sea cables that power the world are made misstated how much it cost to build a vessel, the Monna Lisa. It was about 250 million euros, not 30 million. In addition, the article referred incorrectly to the insulation that encases wires in high-capacity cables. The insulation is made from polyethylene, not polypropylene.

An article on Monday about what to watch on television this week described incorrectly the history of the show “Transplant.” It has been airing on NBC for all seasons; it is not being shown in the United States for the first time.

Because of an editing error, an article on Tuesday about former Gov. Andrew M. Cuomo of New York deciding to run in the general election for New York City mayor referred imprecisely to the political leanings of the cable news network NewsNation. It covers various perspectives, not just conservative ones.

An article on Monday about a new case against Tesla stemming from a 2019 crash of a Tesla Model S sedan with the Autopilot engaged made an incorrect distinction about the trial in Miami. It is the first trial over Tesla’s Autopilot system to reach a federal jury, not the first trial over Autopilot ever to be heard by a jury.
An article on July 9 about the week’s most notable new songs misspelled a trumpeter’s given name. He is Rob Mazurek, not Rod.

An article on Tuesday about the hair loss medication finasteride potentially leading to sperm count reductions misquoted Dr. Kian Asanad, the director of the USC Fertility and Men’s Sexual Health Center, and placed his comments out of context. He said, “I always think about that on the back of my mind as something contributing or playing a role,” and, elsewhere, “I don’t think it’s as common as we really think.” He did not say, “I keep it in the back of my mind as a possible factor, but I would say it’s a less common thing.” In addition, the article omitted funding information related to a 1999 study that found no effects from finasteride on sperm count. The researchers received support from the pharmaceutical company Merck & Co.
ref_headline: I always think about that on the back of my mind as something contributing or playing a role,

text: A news analysis article on Wednesday about how many Republicans in Congress, following the lead of President Trump, are changing their stance on sending aid to Ukraine referred imprecisely to Representative Troy Nehls’s affiliation with the House Freedom Caucus. He was part of the group but is no longer a member.

An article on Wednesday about how the Justice Department is accelerating its efforts to undo decades of civil service protections intended to insulate the work of law enforcement officials from political interference misstated the name of an independent agency that considers appeals from federal workers about employment actions. It is the Merit Systems Protection Board, not the Merit Service Protection Board.

An article on Thursday about the return of a looted mosaic to the city of Pompeii referred imprecisely to some of the volcanic matter Pompeii was buried in when Mount Vesuvius erupted. It was buried in ash and rock fragments, not lava.
A video game review on Thursday about Donkey Kong Bananza misidentified the actress voicing Pauline. She is Jenny Kidd, not Kate Higgins.

An article on Wednesday about an excavation in Ireland where the remains of hundreds of children are thought to have been buried described incorrectly the location of some disposed bodies. It was an old sewage system, not septic tanks.

An article on Thursday about the Chinese A.I. industry misquoted Jia Haojun, the founder of Deep Principle. He said it would be a huge benefit if the government helped cover “13 to 15 percent” of early-stage research costs, not “10 to 15 percent.”

An article on Monday about the biotech company Sarepta Therapeutics defying the Food and Drug Administration’s request that it halt distribution of its treatment for a deadly muscle-wasting disease misstated the subset of Duchenne patients for whom the treatment was first approved. It was approved for those who do not use wheelchairs, not those who do.
An article on Thursday about changes to the Williamstown Theater Festival, using information from the festival, misstated last year’s budget. It was $5.7 million, not $4.7 million.

An article on Sunday about the actor and singer Mandy Patinkin and his family incorrectly misstated the number of Tony Awards that Mr. Patinkin has won. While he was nominated multiple times, he has won only one, for “Evita.”

An article on Sunday about the legal implications of being an organ donor misstated the number of Americans who are in the national donor registry. There are more than 173 million Americans in both state registries and the national registry, not the national registry alone.

An article on Monday about the biotech company Sarepta Therapeutics and its distribution of a treatment for a deadly muscle-wasting disease misstated the date that Sarepta halted shipments of Elevidys for non-ambulatory Duchenne patients. Shipments were halted on June 15, not July 15.

An article on Tuesday about President Trump urging two professional sports teams to change their names misstated the number of carved figures, known as the Guardians of Traffic, on the Hope Memorial Bridge in Cleveland. There are eight, not two.

An obituary on Tuesday about the actor Kenneth Colley misstated the cause of the character Admiral Piett’s death in the movie “Return of the Jedi.” His ship crashes into the Death Star; he is not shot down by a Rebel fighter.

A map with an article on Friday about recent military drills by China’s aircraft carriers in the Pacific reversed the colors of two carriers in the map’s key. The Shandong was in Hong Kong, not the Liaoning.

An obituary on Wednesday about the singer Ozzy Osbourne misstated the location of an incident that led to his being banned from performing in San Antonio. It happened at the Alamo Cenotaph, a memorial just outside the walls of the Alamo — not at the Alamo itself. The obituary also misstated the original surname of Mr. Osbourne’s mother. It was Unitt, not Levy.

Because of an editing error, an obituary on Monday about the Olympic runner and track coach Bill Dellinger misidentified one of the standout runners he coached. He is Matt Centrowitz, an Olympian who won four United States national championships in the 5,000-meter run from 1979 to 1982, not his son, Matthew Centrowitz Jr., who won the gold medal in the 1,500 meters at the 2016 Olympics in Rio de Janeiro.

An article on July 18 about a federal judge’s move to block a rule intended to make it easier for many Americans to get loans by removing medical debt from credit reports referred imprecisely to a part of Judge Sean D. Jordan’s decision. Judge Jordan wrote that state bans on reporting medical debts to credit agencies would be pre-empted by the federal Fair Credit Reporting Act, but he did not issue a ruling pre-empting those bans.

An article on Thursday about Canada’s measles outbreak misstated which officials were urging the Alberta provincial leader to declare a public health emergency. They were public health experts, not provincial officials.

An article on Friday about the powerful earthquake that struck off the coast of Russia’s Kamchatka Peninsula early Wednesday misstated the energy difference between earthquake magnitudes. A magnitude 9 quake has about three times as much energy as magnitude 8.7, not 10 times as much. It has about twice as much energy as magnitude 8.8, not three times as much.

Because of an editing error, an article on Saturday about the impact of President Trump’s shifting tariff levels on the African nation of Lesotho misstated the day Lesotho’s 15 percent tariff rate was announced. It was Thursday night, not Friday night.

An article on Monday about a city in Kansas suing over a planned ICE detention center misstated the language in a poster seen at a protest of an immigration detention facility in Leavenworth, Kan. The poster said that Leavenworth is “more than a prison town,” rather than “not just a prison town.”

An article on Friday about Ford Motor announcing that it lost money in the second quarter as tariffs took a toll on its business misstated the day that Ford reported its second-quarter earnings. It was Wednesday, not Tuesday.

A picture from the streaming outlet TBPN published with an article on Friday about A.I. researchers’ pay packages misidentified a Microsoft employee who used to work at Google’s DeepMind lab. The person shown in the image was not Amar Subramanya.

An article on Saturday about the negative impact that the Trump administration’s tariffs are having on businesses they were meant to help misstated the month that the United States lost 11,000 manufacturing jobs. It was July, not June. The article also misstated the number of manufacturing job losses in June, based on initial estimates. The revised number was 15,000, not 6,000. The earlier estimate was 6,000.

An article on Sunday about a veteran lifeguard’s Friday routine misstated, in some instances, Javier Rodriguez’s surname on second reference and that of his three adult children. Their surname is Rodriguez, not Hernandez.
An article on Sunday about the Vatican’s six-day celebration of youth in the church referred incorrectly to some of the festivities. They were part of the Jubilee of Youth, not World Youth Day.

An article on Monday about what to watch on television this week misstated the premiere time of “American Prince: J.F.K. Jr.” on CNN. It will premiere at 9 p.m. Eastern on Saturday, Aug. 9, not 10 p.m.

An article on Tuesday about jurists losing faith in President Trump’s Justice Department misstated Andrew Wiederhorn’s role with the restaurant chain Fatburger. He was previously a chief executive of the chain and founded its parent company. He did not create the restaurant chain.

A picture caption with an article on Wednesday about a growing debate in Japan between those who defend pacifism as a national virtue and those who think the country must abandon it referred incorrectly to a treaty that Japan has not signed. It is the Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons, not the Nuclear Nonproliferation Treaty.

An article on Wednesday about how the Trump administration will gain access to the standardized test scores and grade point averages of all applicants to Columbia and Brown University misstated the findings of an SAT report from the College Board. The report found that 1 percent of the African American high school graduates who took the SAT in 2024 scored between 1400 and 1600, the highest possible scores, and 27 percent of the Asian graduates scored at that level. It is not the case that, of all the high school graduates who scored between 1400 and 1600 in 2024, 1 percent were African American and 27 percent were Asian.

An article on July 31 about Brazil’s President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva voicing frustration with President Trump over tariffs misidentified birds roaming the presidential palace lawns. They were rheas, not emus.

An article on Wednesday about how Texas Republicans are hoping the surge of Hispanic support for President Trump in 2024 will last through the 2026 midterm elections misstated the percentage of votes that Representative Henry Cuellar, Democrat of Texas, won in Starr County in the 2024 elections. It was 69 percent, not 60 percent.

An article on Wednesday about the Trump administration’s plans to terminate $7 billion in federal grants intended to help low- and moderate-income families install solar panels on their homes misstated the amount of money the Georgia Bright Communities Coalition was using to provide free rooftop solar panels to about 800 Georgia households. It is about $12 million of a $156 million Solar for All grant, not the entire $156 million grant amount.

An article on Monday about businesses rushing goods out of duty-free warehouses to bonded warehouses, allowing foreign companies a way to postpone the payment of tariffs until the goods are sent to stores or directly to customers, misidentified James Marley of Swap Commerce. He is the head of operations at Swap Commerce, not the chief executive.

An article on Wednesday about ESPN’s new streaming service starting misstated the stakes of Disney and Hearst in ESPN. Disney will own 72 percent of ESPN, not 70 percent, and Hearst will own 18 percent, not 20 percent.

An article on Tuesday about political historian Heather Cox Richardson preparing for the narrator role in Aaron Copland’s “Lincoln Portrait” misstated her surname. It is Richardson, not Cox Richardson.

An article on Tuesday about Representative Nancy Mace announcing her run for governor of South Carolina referred incorrectly to President Trump’s primary victory in South Carolina. The state was his second primary victory in 2016, not his first.

An article on Thursday about the Royal Ballet and Opera canceling a collaboration with the Israeli Opera to stage “Tosca” in Tel Aviv next year misidentified the opera at which a dancer staged a protest during the curtain call. It was “Il Trovatore,” not “La Traviata.”

A picture caption with an obituary on July 19 about the editorial cartoonist Steve Benson misstated the given name of a former governor of Arizona, whom he often lampooned. As the obituary correctly notes, he was Evan Mecham, not Alfred.
An obituary on July 28 about the songwriter Tom Lehrer misstated his mother’s given name. It was Anna, not Alma.

An obituary on Saturday about James A. Lovell Jr., the commander of the three-man Apollo 13 spacecraft that survived a near-catastrophic explosion as it approached the moon in April 1970, misstated how much time elapsed before Apollo 13 detected signs of trouble. It was 56 hours, not 56 minutes. It also misidentified the astronaut who was to accompany him to the surface of the moon in a lunar module, though that mission was never accomplished. He was Fred W. Haise Jr., not John L. Swigert Jr. Mr. Swigert was to remain behind in the orbiting Apollo 13 spaceship. The obituary also misstated how many great-grandchildren Captain Lovell had. It is nine, not eight.

An article and related map on Aug. 1 about the countries that recognize a Palestinian state, relying on a U.N. document from April 2024, misstated the Czech Republic’s position on the recognition of a Palestinian state. While Czechoslovakia recognized Palestinian statehood in 1988, the Czech Republic recognizes the goal of an independent Palestinian state, but does not currently recognize it as one.

An article on Thursday about Britain’s charities’ regulator clearing Prince Harry of bullying, harassment and sexism at the charity he co-founded misstated the year that Sophie Chandauka became a trustee of Sentebale. It was 2008, not 2009. Because of an editing error, the article also misstated the number of trustees left on the Sentebale board after the resignations on March 24. Five trustees resigned, leaving only Ms. Chandauka on the board, not five of nine. Ms. Chandauka appointed four new trustees on March 25.

An article on Monday about the announcement that the Row NYC in Times Square will stop operating as a housing shelter for migrants misstated the ownership of the Row NYC. Rockport Group, a Boston-based real estate private equity firm, no longer has a stake in the hotel.
An article on Monday about the weekend’s box office earnings misstated the rating of the “Naked Gun” remake. It is PG-13, not R.

An article on Saturday about the music label Penrose Records and its influence in a soft-touch style of soul music misspelled the surname of an artist on the label’s roster. She is Vicky Tafoya, not Trafoya.
An article on Tuesday about Quebec City’s small English-speaking community misspelled the name of a photographer and filmmaker. She is Sandra Tomaylla, not Sandra Tomayla.

An article on Aug. 3 about corporations and individuals, including many in the crypto business, that wrote big checks to MAGA Inc. while seeking favor from President Trump misstated the total amount of money that MAGA Inc. received from crypto interests during the first half of this year, as well as the combined amount donated by Tyler and Cameron Winklevoss and their crypto exchange, Gemini. The total amount that MAGA Inc. received from crypto interests was nearly $42 million, not nearly $45 million. The Winklevosses combined to donate more than $1 million, not nearly $4 million. Their crypto exchange, Gemini, processed donations to MAGA Inc. made by other donors; it did not donate its own funds.

An article on Friday about the ways that Republicans shaped the debate over Sydney Sweeney’s American Eagle ad campaign misstated the date of one of Libs of TikTok’s social media posts on the American Eagle ad. While attention did shift with earlier posts by the influential account on July 27, the “Keep this up Democrats” comment cited in the article was published on July 28, not July 27.

An article on Wednesday about the shooting outside a Target store in Austin, Texas, on Monday, misstated, using information from an initial police report, the name of the Target employee who died in the attack. The name was Rosa Machuca, not Hector Leopoldo Martinez Machuca. (The article was updated after the police released an update on the employee’s name.)

An article this weekend on Page 120 about censorship of creative works contains several errors. It refers incorrectly to the artist Dread Scott’s 1989 installation “What Is the Proper Way to Display a U.S. Flag?” The exhibit offered visitors the choice of stepping on a flag while answering that question in a guest book; it did not require them to step on the flag. The article misstates the number of photographs of soldiers’ coffins draped with American flags in the installation. There was one such image, not multiple images. The article also misstates the artist Andres Serrano’s number of solo exhibitions in his career. He’s had about 20 solo museum exhibitions in Europe and abroad, and many fewer in the United States — not about 20 solo in Europe and only one in the United States. And because of a production error, the article misquotes John Waters on what a Maryland film censor said to him about his 1974 feature “Female Trouble.” Waters said she told him, “You can’t have that cunnilingus scene,” not “You can have that cunnilingus scene.”
ref_headline: What Is the Proper Way to Display a U.S. Flag?

text: A picture caption with an article this weekend on Page 140 about the artist Robert Longo, relying on information from a publicist, misstates the materials used in one of his works. It is a charcoal drawing of a pit of snakes, not an ink-and-charcoal drawing.

text: A picture caption with an article this weekend on Page 140 about the artist Robert Longo, relying on information from a publicist, misstates the materials used in one of his works. It is a charcoal drawing of a pit of snakes, not an ink-and-charcoal drawing.

A news analysis article on Saturday about President Trump’s summit meeting with President Vladimir V. Putin and the way it marked a return to imperial thinking, citing an expert, misstated the time frame for three divisions of Poland in the 18th century. It was between 1772 and 1795, not 1792 and 1795.

An article on Sunday about the indictment of LaToya Cantrell, the mayor of New Orleans, for her actions related to a romantic relationship she had with a city police officer assigned to protect her, misidentified the New Orleans television station that staked out a city-owned apartment in the French Quarter. It was WVUE-TV, not WWL-TV.

An article on Sunday about the scrutiny that Representative Rob Bresnahan Jr. of Pennsylvania is facing over his stock trades referred incorrectly to the day of Mr. Bresnahan’s wedding. It was last Saturday, not next Saturday.

An obituary on Sunday about Robin Lakoff, a linguist who analyzed the ways women speak, referred incorrectly to the connection between Dr. Lakoff’s writings and a best-selling book by Deborah Tannen, “You Just Don’t Understand: Women and Men in Conversation.” The book did not popularize some of Dr. Lakoff’s ideas; rather, its subject matter, language and gender were a field that Dr. Lakoff inspired.
ref_headline: You Just Don’t Understand: Women and Men in Conversation.

text: An obituary on Monday about the art collector and critic Doris Lockhart Saatchi misstated the number of years she was married to Charles Saatchi. It was 17, not 14.

Because of a production error, an article on Sunday about why the U.S. government is offering a $5 million reward for information leading to the arrest or conviction of a high-profile Haitian gang leader was missing the full name and identification of a man arrested in Texas in July. He is Bazile Richardson, a Haitian American trucker in North Carolina.

An article on Tuesday about the International Energy Agency forecasts suggesting that global demand for oil and gas could peak by the end of the decade misspelled the surname of the European Union energy minister. He is Dan Jorgensen, not Jorgenson.

Because of an editing error, an article on Monday about potential red flags that have popped up this summer heading into the next N.B.A. season misstated the number of red flags. There are eight, not 10.

An article on Saturday about factory-built houses that are the latest attempt by FEMA to address a longtime need to upgrade temporary emergency housing misspelled the given name of the director of operations of Liv-Connected. She is Allysa Taylor, not Allyssa.
An article on Aug. 2 about Edinburgh’s Festival Fringe described incorrectly the ’70s band that features in “Philosophy of the World.” The band, the Shaggs, was a real band, not a fictional one.

An article on Thursday about Israel approving new settlements in the West Bank and announcing that it was moving ahead with plans to take over Gaza City misspelled the surname of an Israeli analyst and former military intelligence officer. He is Michael Milshtein, not Milstein.

An article on Wednesday about the African Union endorsing a campaign to have organizations around the world replace the Mercator map with other alternatives referred imprecisely to the nationality of Gerardus Mercator. He was from what is now Belgium, not Germany.

An obituary on Monday about the actor Terence Stamp misstated when he entered an ashram. It was in the middle or late 1970s, not 1969. The obituary also referred incorrectly to “Billy Budd,” by Herman Melville, which was the basis of a 1962 movie starring Mr. Stamp. It is a novella, not a novel.

An obituary on Wednesday about the political reporter and columnist Jules Witcover misidentified the New Jersey high school he attended. It was Union Hill High School — not Union City High School, which was created when two schools, Emerson and Union Hill, merged in 2008.
An article on Sunday about how Michael Boulos, husband of Tiffany Trump, benefited financially from marrying into the Trump family referred incorrectly to Mar-a-Lago. It is a club, not a golf club.

An article on Monday about the Coast Guard’s removal of buoys along the Northeast misspelled the given name of the Pilgrim leader who nearly hit the Mary Ann Rocks in December 1620. He was Myles Standish, not Miles.

An article on Tuesday about efforts to build parks along Lower Manhattan’s waterfront referred imprecisely to proposed alternatives to a senior housing development in the area. While the alternatives remain uncertain, it is not the case that no developer has expressed interest in them.

An article on Monday about the top 25 potential M.L.B. free agents for 2025-26 referred incorrectly to a pitcher’s league affiliation. Framber Valdez of the Houston Astros finished in the top 10 in Cy Young Award voting in each of the past three seasons in the American League, not the National League.

An article about President Trump’s heading overseas to persuade other countries to choose fossil fuels over renewable energy sources described incorrectly the setting in which Chris Wright, the energy secretary, called net-zero targets a “sinister goal.” He made the remarks remotely, via video — not in person in London.

An article about federal funding cuts affecting American history content on PBS referred incorrectly to the series “Independent Lens,” produced by ITVS. The show co-produces most of the documentaries it airs; it does not exclusively acquire already completed films.
A picture caption with an article on Friday about how few people remain in Paris during the month of August misidentified an island in the River Seine. It is Île de la Cité, not Île St.-Louis.

An article on Thursday about a film series at the Museum of Modern Art marking the 20th anniversary of Hurricane Katrina misstated the number of fatalities attributable to the disaster. It is 1,392, not 1,833. The article also misidentified the setting of the film “The Beasts of the Southern Wild.” The film takes place in a fictional setting inspired in part by New Orleans’s Lower Ninth Ward, not literally in the Ninth Ward.
ref_headline: The Beasts of the Southern Wild.

text: A review on Thursday for the film “The Roses” misidentified the California community to which the couple moves. It is Mendocino, not Montecito.
A review on Friday for the film “The Thursday Murder Club” misidentified the agency that the character Elizabeth works for. It is MI6, not MI5.
An article on Friday about what to see in New York City galleries misstated the name of the gallery where Jane Dickson’s work is on view. It is Karma, not Karma Karma.

An article on page 12 this weekend about the de Young Museum in San Francisco reinstalling its Native art galleries misstated the reason 12 positions were eliminated at the Fine Arts Museums of San Francisco. The 12 were cut as a result of increased costs and declines in attendance, not because of a reduction in municipal funding.

A picture caption with an article on page 20 this weekend about children of Syrian detainees who were placed in orphanages by the government of Bashar al-Assad misidentified the orphanage shown in the photograph. It is a SOS Children’s Villages care center in Qudssaya, not the Lahn al-Hayat orphanage.

An article on page 12 this weekend about the de Young Museum in San Francisco reinstalling its Native art galleries misstated the reason 12 positions were eliminated at the Fine Arts Museums of San Francisco. The 12 were cut as a result of increased costs and declines in attendance, not because of a reduction in municipal funding.

A picture caption with an article on page 20 this weekend about children of Syrian detainees who were placed in orphanages by the government of Bashar al-Assad misidentified the orphanage shown in the photograph. It is a SOS Children’s Villages care center in Qudssaya, not the Lahn al-Hayat orphanage.
An article on Sunday about the Lore bathing club opening in Manhattan misstates the cost of a monthly pass. It is $200, not $179. (The price changed after the article went to press.)
Because of an editing error, an article on Sunday about bed and mattress shopping inadvertently referred to cotton as an animal material rather than a plant.
An obituary on Aug. 15 about the actress Danielle Spencer misstated how many episodes of the television show “What’s Happening!!” she appeared in. It was 63, not 65.

An obituary on Aug. 16 about the lawyer Gerry Spence referred imprecisely to the subject of his book “The Martyrdom of Collins Catch the Bear.” Although Mr. Spence wrote that Mr. Catch the Bear was wrongfully charged with murder, he, in fact, pleaded guilty to second-degree manslaughter.
ref_headline: The Martyrdom of Collins Catch the Bear.

text: An obituary on Monday about the writer Joan Mellen referred incorrectly to the novelist Kay Boyle, whose biography she wrote. She was not Southern.

An article on Tuesday about the military-grade weapons that Mexico’s most powerful criminal groups are amassing misstated the name of the leader of an anti-bomb unit in Michoacán. He is Capt. Carlos Gómez, not Mario.

An article on Monday about the deadly floods in Pakistan’s eastern Punjab Province misstated the name of Pakistan’s disaster management body. It is the National Disaster Management Authority, not Agency.

An article on Tuesday about the more than 445,000 federal employees who saw their union protections disappear in August misidentified the location of a veterans’ medical center employing Irma Westmoreland, a nurse and union official. The medical center is in Augusta, Ga., not Atlanta.

An article on Aug. 25 about Major League Baseball’s top 25 potential free agents in the 2025-26 class misidentified the league that Houston Astros starting pitcher Framber Valdez plays in. It is the American League, not the National League.

An article on Wednesday about Belgium’s decision to recognize a Palestinian state at this month’s United Nations General Assembly misstated the month that President Emmanuel Macron of France announced that the country would recognize a Palestinian state. It was July, not August.

A picture caption with an article on Aug. 29 about the news that The Atlanta Journal-Constitution will stop publishing a print newspaper at the end of the year misstated the year of an archived edition of the paper shown in the image. The edition was from 1954, not 1942.

An article on Monday about a pro-Palestinian demonstration that took place during the 82nd Venice International Film Festival on Saturday misstated the death toll cited by the Gaza Health Ministry. The ministry recently put the figure at more than 60,000 people, not over 39,000.

A picture caption with an article on Sunday about healthy gardening misidentified two types of plants. The blue one is Love-in-a-Mist, or Nigella damascena, not fennel flower, and the red plant is Cardinal flower, or Lobelia cardinalis, not Lobelia tupa.

An article on Aug. 28 about Australia’s accusation that Iran directed an attack on a Jewish restaurant in Sydney referred incorrectly to statements made at a pro-Palestine rally at the Sydney Opera House on Oct. 9, 2023. Local officials concluded that some “offensive and completely unacceptable” statements against Jews were made; they did not conclude that no such statements were made.
An article on Aug. 29 about the mystery behind a missing painting misstated the century in which “Portrait of a Lady” was painted. It was the 18th century, not the 17th.
An article on Thursday about the Off Broadway show “The Brothers Size” misidentified a character that André Holland played in the production. He was Elegba, not Oshoosi.
An obituary on Saturday about the civil rights lawyer James E. Ferguson II misstated when he retired from the law firm Ferguson, Chambers & Sumter. It was in 2024, not 2014.

An article on Friday about a U.S. warning that Lebanon’s leaders are running out of time to disarm the militant group Hezbollah gave an outdated title and affiliation for Firas Maksad. He is the managing director for the Middle East and North Africa at Eurasia Group, not an associate fellow at the Middle East Institute.

An article on Thursday about how a new generation of animal activists has used extreme tactics and viral video to challenge farmers in Sonoma County, a region in California known for some of the industry’s most humane practices, misidentified the number of years that Zoe Rosenberg faces in jail if she is convicted of the nighttime raid at Petaluma Poultry. She faces up to four and a half years, not six.

An article on Friday about the arraignment of a man accused of killing two young employees of the Israeli Embassy in Washington in May gave an incorrect title for Benjamin Netanyahu. He is the prime minister of Israel, not the president.
An article on Sept. 1 about what to watch on television this week misidentified the MTV Video Music Awards statuette. It is a moon person, not a moon man.
An article this weekend on Page 18 about cheeseburgers misstates the name of an East Village park. It is Tompkins Square Park, not Thompson Square Park.
An article this weekend on Page 18 about cheeseburgers misstates the name of an East Village park. It is Tompkins Square Park, not Thompson Square Park.

An article on Saturday about the debate over an unproven link between acetaminophen, the active ingredient in the painkiller Tylenol, causing problems in children like autism and A.D.H.D. misstated the title of Brian Lee of Drexel University, the lead author of the study. He is a professor of epidemiology, not an associate professor.

An article on Sunday about the hundreds of L.G.B.T.Q. Catholics who participated in the church’s quarter-centennial festival of renewal and forgiveness on Saturday misstated the Catholic Church’s teaching on homosexual acts. The church considers them “intrinsically disordered,” not “intrinsically distorted.”

A headline with an article on Aug. 30 about concerns over Emil Bove’s continued work at the Justice Department after his confirmation as an appeals court judge misstated how the code of conduct for federal judges applies to him. As the article correctly noted, Mr. Bove has yet to be sworn in, and is therefore not bound by the code. It is not the case that he has disregarded it.
An article on Friday about the newly restored film “Linda Linda Linda” misspelled the band name in the movie. The group is Paranmaum, not Parlan Maum.

A news analysis article on Monday about the ways the Trump administration has undercut the legislative branch misstated the year in which the Foreign Malign Influence Center, created by Congress to counter election interference efforts, played a major role warning about Russian propaganda in a presidential campaign. It was 2024, not 2020.

An article on Tuesday about the Trump administration’s announcement that it had begun a crackdown on illegal immigration in Chicago on Monday misidentified the organization where Tricia McLaughlin works. She is the assistant secretary for public affairs at the Department of Homeland Security, not Immigration and Customs Enforcement.

Because of an editing error, an article on Tuesday about the season debuts of N.F.L. quarterbacks who changed teams in the past year misstated, in one instance, the age of the Pittsburgh Steelers quarterback Aaron Rodgers. He is 41, not 42.

An article on Sunday about the Atlantic Coast leopard frog referred imprecisely to the team of biologists that identified the species. They were affiliated with several universities, not solely Rutgers University.

An article on Sunday about the return of the remains of 42 people found outside a now-abandoned ethnic Polish village in western Ukraine after World War II misstated the location of the mass graves. They are in western Ukraine, not western Poland.

An article on Wednesday about the search for a man suspected of killing a married couple in Queens and setting their house on fire, relying on information from the police, misstated the ages of the victims. The man was 76 and the woman 77, not 77 and 78.

An article on Thursday about the delay in repatriation of hundreds of South Korean workers arrested in an immigration raid in the United States last week, using information from a lawyer, misstated the nationalities of his clients. They included people from Colombia, Japan and Mexico, not only those from South Korea.

An article on Tuesday about the ways that neurologists are exploring medications that would help the brain recover after a stroke or traumatic injury misstated the professional title of Dr. Einor Ben Assayag. She is a geneticist, not a neurologist.
An article this weekend on Page 48 about the American photographer and memoirist Sally Mann misstates the year of Mann’s forthcoming show at Gagosian Gallery. It is planned for 2027, not 2026.

An article this weekend on Page 44 about “Monuments,” a landmark exhibition at the Geffen Contemporary at the Museum of Contemporary Art in Los Angeles that combines decommissioned statues of Confederate and other figures with works by 18 contemporary artists, misspells the name of an opera singer featured in a film that will be shown at the exhibition. He is Davóne Tines, not Tynes.

An article this weekend on Page 26 about JPMorgan’s ties to Jeffrey Epstein misstates the chronology of Mr. Epstein’s early career. He was a high school math teacher before working on Wall Street, not after.
An obituary on Friday about the activist Charlie Kirk misidentified the beauty pageant that his wife won. It was Miss Arizona USA, not Miss Arizona.
An article this weekend on Page 48 about the American photographer and memoirist Sally Mann misstates the year of Mann’s forthcoming show at Gagosian Gallery. It is 2027, not 2026.

An article this weekend on Page 44 about “Monuments,” a landmark exhibition at the Geffen Contemporary at the Museum of Contemporary Art in Los Angeles that combines decommissioned statues of Confederate and other figures with works by 18 contemporary artists, misspells the name of an opera singer featured in a film that will be shown in the exhibition. He is Davóne Tines, not Tynes.

An article this weekend on Page 26 about JPMorgan’s ties to Jeffrey Epstein misstates the chronology of Mr. Epstein’s early career. He was a high school math teacher before working on Wall Street, not after.

An article on Sunday about Secretary of State Marco Rubio traveling to Israel over the weekend to consult with Israeli officials about their planned military offensive in Gaza City misstated the length of the war in Gaza. It began nearly two years ago, not nearly three years.
A Critic’s Notebook article on Friday about the conceptual artist and musician Stephen Prina misstated the age at which Mr. Prina scored a Mozart string quartet. He was 21, not 22.

An obituary on Sept. 10 about the internationally renowned conductor Christoph von Dohnanyi referred incorrectly to Mr. Dohnanyi’s appointment as chief conductor of the Frankfurt Opera in Germany. He took the helm in 1968, several years after Georg Solti stepped down from that position; it was not the case that he directly succeeded Solti. Two other conductors — Lovro von Matacic and Theodore Bloomfield — preceded Mr. Dohnanyi after Solti’s departure.

Because of an editing error, an article on Saturday about reactions from pharmaceutical companies and prominent investors to proposed anti-China measures misstated when Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick made comments about several types of medicines that are believed to have substantial production in China in a CNBC interview. The interview was on Sept. 5, a week earlier, not Sept. 12, the day before.

Because of an editing error, an article on Monday about efforts to protect workers from the dangers of heat stress misstated the Fahrenheit conversion of 31 degrees Celsius. It is 87.8 degrees, not 107 degrees.

An article on Saturday about acute necrotizing encephalopathy, a neurological condition that can result from the flu or other infections, misstated the date on which Olivia Yanxi Li, the 4-year-old who developed A.N.E., was sent home from school with a fever. It was Jan. 23, not Jan. 21.

An article on Sept. 7 about Jonathan Kleisner’s career transition from commodities trader to paramedic misidentified the country from which an 11-year-old girl and her family — all of whom died in the fire at the Twin Parks North West residential tower in the Bronx — emigrated. It was Gambia, not Guyana.

An article on Wednesday about Tim Walz’s announcement that he is seeking a third term as governor of Minnesota included an outdated estimate of Minnesota’s potential budget deficit. State budget officials in July projected that Minnesota would face a shortfall in 2028 of $1.1 billion, not nearly $6 billion, as the officials had projected earlier in the year before lawmakers made cuts.
An article on Sunday about the increase in political discourse among online hobbyists misspelled the surname of the host of The Guitologist channel on YouTube. He is Brad Linzy, not Lindzy.

An obituary on Saturday about the sculptor Robert Grosvenor misstated the size of a solar telescope that he said inspired his first major piece. It was 110 feet high, not 30. The error was repeated in a picture caption.

An article on Friday about a man from Texas who was charged with terroristic threat and aggravated harassment against Zohran Mamdani misstated the name of the Texas county where the man was held in jail before being brought to New York to face charges. It is Collin, not Collins.

An article on Thursday about a shooting in southern Pennsylvania in which three police officers were killed referred incorrectly to the families with whom Governor Shapiro met on Wednesday. They were the families of the officers who were injured in the shooting, not those of the officers who were killed.
An obituary on Sept. 12 about the activist Charlie Kirk misstated his mother’s given name. It is Kathryn, not Kimberley.

A picture caption with an article on Thursday about the royal welcome that Britain extended to President Trump on a recent visit referred incorrectly to Charles. He is, of course, the king, not a prince.
An article on Sunday about the Russian drone incursion in Poland misspelled the surname of a Polish Defense Ministry official. He is Pawel Zalewski, not Zalevski.

An article on Sunday about new restrictions imposing by the Pentagon on reporters covering the Department of Defense misstated the number of press briefings Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has held. He has held a handful of briefings, not one.

An article on Sunday about the future of the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program misstated the name of an Illinois state department. It is the Department of Human Services, not the Department of Human Resources.

An article on Sunday about Pipeline Fund, a left-leaning group that is vying to establish itself as a liberal counter to the sprawling down-ballot infrastructure on the right that has helped Republicans notch wins in local races, using information from the Pipeline Fund, misstated the one of the states the group is expanding to. It is Massachusetts, not Connecticut.

An article on Monday about Elon Musk redoubling his efforts with xAI since his falling out with President Trump in June misspelled the surname of an xAI founder. He is Igor Babuschkin, not Babuskin or Babushkin.

An obituary on Saturday about Agnes Gund, an art collector and philanthropist who served as president of the Museum of Modern Art for over a decade, misstated Glenn D. Lowry’s association with the Museum of Modern Art. He stepped down as director this month, shortly before Ms. Gund died; he is not the current director. The obituary also referred incorrectly to Ms. Gund’s sale of a Roy Lichtenstein painting, “Mirror #5.” It sold for $3.8 million, her family said, not $3.1 million, and Ms. Gund donated the proceeds to 29 organizations that advocate for reproductive rights, not one such group.

An article on Tuesday about a judge’s ruling on Monday that permits a Danish energy company to restart work on Revolution Wind, a large wind farm off the coast of Rhode Island that had been abruptly halted last month by the Trump administration misspelled, in some instances, the surname of the judge who granted the injunction. He is Royce Lamberth, not Lambert.

An article on Tuesday about an underwater gas pipeline that had been rejected three times in New York misstated Ken Lovett’s title. He is the governor’s communications adviser for energy and environment, not for energy and efficiency.

An article on Sept. 19 about the Trump administration’s announcement on the launch of a civics education coalition that includes the Education Department and more than 40 partner groups, all of which are closely aligned with the president’s Make America Great Again movement, misstated the circumstances around the founding of the Center for Civic Education. It traces its roots to planning for the bicentennial of the U.S. Constitution, not the nation’s bicentennial.

An article on Sept. 17 about Sotheby’s acquisition of a Leonard Lauder collection valued at more than $400 million that will be auctioned in November misidentified the year that “Blooming Meadow” was created, in one instance. It was 1906, not 1908.

An article on Wednesday about a new exhibition in Italy spotlighting the Renaissance painter Fra Angelico misidentified the religious community in which Angelico he served. He entered an order of Dominican friars, not monks.

An article on Wednesday about Lindsey Halligan, the lawyer chosen by President Trump to replace the ousted U.S. attorney in the Eastern District of Virginia, misidentified the group for which Ms. Halligan once interned for a semester in 2013. She worked at the Miami Innocence Clinic, which specializes in fighting wrongful convictions and is affiliated with the Innocence Network, not at the Innocence Project.

An article on Wednesday about the death of Abdulaziz Al Asheikh, Saudi Arabia’s most senior cleric, misstated Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s role in a group prayer in Riyadh held in honor of the grand mufti who died on Tuesday. He participated in a funeral prayer, he did not lead it.

Because of an editing error, an article on Friday about the U.S. Customs and Border Protection’s announcement regarding the detention of bicycles, parts, and accessories manufactured in Taiwan by Giant Manufacturing Co. Ltd., following an investigation that found evidence of forced labor, incorrectly described Walmart’s relationship with Giant. The retailer does not sell Giant bikes.
An article this weekend on Page 6 about the opening of Gladys Books & Wine in Bedford-Stuyvesant, Brooklyn, misstates the age of Tiffany Dockery, the shop’s owner. She is 37 years old, not 39.

An article on Aug. 31 about protests over debris from the Palisades and Eaton fires being taken to the Calabasas, Calif., landfill misidentified Kendra Wilkinson. She appeared as a model in Playboy magazine; she was not a Playmate of the Month.
An article this weekend on Page 6 about the opening of Gladys Books & Wine in Bedford-Stuyvesant, Brooklyn, misstates the age of Tiffany Dockery, the shop’s owner. She is 37 years old, not 39.

An article on Aug. 31 about protests over debris from the Palisades and Eaton fires being taken to the Calabasas, Calif., landfill misidentified Kendra Wilkinson. She appeared as a model in Playboy magazine; she was not a Playmate of the Month.
An obituary on Sept. 17 about the actor Robert Redford referred incorrectly to his tenure at the University of Colorado. He did not attend on a baseball scholarship.

An article on Monday about the uncertainty federal workers are facing with a possible government shutdown this week misstated the name of a liberal think tank focused on inequality. It is the Washington Center for Equitable Growth, not the Washington Center for Equitable Health.

An article on Thursday about public health advocates warning of the shortcomings of a U.N. declaration on chronic diseases mistakenly included the Distilled Spirits Council of the United States among the industry groups that issued statements praising the process and outcome of the U.N. declaration. The organization has not commented on the final document.

A picture caption with an article on Saturday about Cleveland Browns defensive tackle Shelby Harris’s penchant for blocking field goal attempts described incorrectly the action in one of the photographs. While one image showed Harris celebrating after blocking a kick against the Green Bay Packers on Sept. 21, the other image was of him blocking a kick for another team in 2020; it was not from the Browns-Packers game.

An obituary on Friday about Sara Jane Moore, who attempted to assassinate President Gerald R. Ford in 1975, misspelled the surname of Stephen Sondheim’s collaborator on the musical “Assassins.” He is John Weidman, not Wideman.

An obituary on Monday about Russell M. Nelson, the oldest serving president of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, misstated the year that Mr. Nelson was appointed to the Quorum of Twelve Apostles, the Latter-day Saints’ second-highest governing body. It was 1984, not 1985.

An article on Sept. 8 about Chloë Bass’s new audio-based public art project that will be heard over the P.A. system at 14 M.T.A. stations around New York attributed an erroneous distinction to the languages spoken in New York City that were used in subway announcements. They are six of the top 10 languages spoken in the city, but not the top six.

A headline with an article on Oct. 1 about President Trump’s initiative to lower drug prices misstated the federal health insurance program that would be affected. The president’s plan involves Medicaid, not Medicare.

An obituary on Monday about Russell N. Nelson, the president of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, referred incorrectly to the military hospital where he was stationed during the Korean War. It was called the Walter Reed Army Medical Center at the time, not the Walter Reed National Military Medical Center, the name it adopted after merging with the National Naval Medical Center in 2011.

An obituary on Thursday about the primatologist Jane Goodall misstated the number of chapters the Jane Goodall Institute has around the world — it has 25, not 34 — and it misstated the number of countries where it sponsors its Roots and Shoots program, which teaches young people about conservation. The program is provided in 75 countries, not 120. The obituary also referred incorrectly to Dr. Goodall’s public speaking in her last decades. She had traveled some 300 days a year on speaking tours, her institute said; she did not make 300 speaking appearances a year.

An article on Thursday about the partial collapse of a Bronx building on Wednesday misstated the estimated cost of repairs and improvements needed in the New York City public housing system over the next 20 years. It is $80 billion, not $80 million.

An article on Monday about a new museum in Mexico City showcasing the artist Frida Kahlo’s family and origins misidentified the substance that was used on Kahlo in her surgeries. It was chloroform, not formaldehyde.
An article on Tuesday about Lionel Richie’s memoir “Truly” misstated the previous name of Tuskegee University. It was Tuskegee Institute, not Tuskegee College.

An obituary on Wednesday about George Hardy, a retired Air Force lieutenant colonel who was one of the last surviving combat veterans of the Tuskegee Airmen, referred incorrectly to one of his sons, Gerald Hardy. He died a few years ago in an automobile accident; he does not survive his father.

An article on Sept. 28 about the family business involvement of Steve Witkoff in the Middle East misstated the source of the Qatari government’s investments in think tanks, universities and lobbying groups. Those expenditures came from other organizations also backed by the Qatari government, not from the country’s sovereign wealth fund.

A news analysis article on Friday about the rising concerns of antisemitism in Britain following the Manchester synagogue attack on Thursday, using information provided by the police, misstated the number of people injured. It was three, not four.

An article on Friday about American comics at Saudi Arabia’s first global comedy festival misstated the title of a podcast on which Jim Jeffries had appeared before the festival. It is “This Past Weekend,” not “This Last Weekend.”

An article on Friday about the Iraqi-born artist Hayv Kahraman’s New York show “Ghost Fires” referred incorrectly to Kahraman’s paintings in her new show. They each have individual names. They don’t share a single title called ‘Untitled (To Be Titled).’”

An article on Tuesday about the shutdown of a North Carolina program that provided nutritious food, safe housing and transportation for doctors’ visits that helped Medicaid recipients misstated the number of people enrolled in North Carolina’s Medicaid pilot program and misattributed the source of the research estimating the pilot’s savings. While there were 13,000 people participating from the state’s western region, there were about 40,000 Medicaid recipients enrolled statewide. The analysis of savings was conducted by a research center at the University of North Carolina, not its medical school.

An article on Sept. 28 about the family business involvement of Steve Witkoff in the Middle East referred imprecisely to the source of $100 million received by the Witkoff Group for the Belgrove property. The lender in that transaction was Apollo Global Management, a private-equity firm that has worked with Qatar, not a financial trust that is a subsidiary of Apollo and partly owned by Qatar.

An obituary on Thursday about the primatologist Jane Goodall referred incorrectly to chimpanzees. They are one of two species that are genetically closest to humans, the other being bonobos. It is not the case that chimpanzees alone have that distinction. The obituary also misstated Dr. Goodall’s date of birth. It was April 3, 1934, not April 4.

An article on Saturday about the scene inside Heaton Park Hebrew Congregation’s synagogue in Manchester, England, during the terrorist attack last Thursday, relying on initial reports from the police that were later updated, misstated the role of Melvin Cravitz, who died in Thursday’s attack. He was a worshiper at the synagogue, not a security guard, and he was outside the building, not inside. (The error was repeated in a separate article on Saturday about the man believed to have carried out the attack.)

Because of an editing error, a picture caption with an article on Saturday about the Trump administration’s emphasis on defense concerns instead of climate research in the rapidly warming Arctic region misspelled the name of a United States Coast Guard ship. It is Healy, not Healey.

A picture caption in an article on Monday about popular destinations and their status during the government shutdown misstated the public access to Joshua Tree National Park in California. It remains open, not closed, according to the park’s website.
A Critic’s Notebook article on Saturday about a new retrospective of Lee Miller’s work at Tate Britain in London misstated when Carolyn Burke first met Miller. They met in 1977, not 1970.

An obituary on Sunday about Phyllis Gardner, who as a pharmacology professor at Stanford told Elizabeth Holmes that her idea for a new blood-testing technology would fail, and later became a critical source in the investigation that led to Ms. Holmes’s conviction in one of the biggest fraud cases in U.S. history, misstated the relationship of Richard Fuisz to Ms. Holmes, the founder of Theranos. He was a friend of Holmes’s family, but was not also an early investor in the company.
An article on Monday about dissidents in Nicaragua misstated the surname of a political prisoner whose body was returned to his family. He is Mauricio Alonso Petri, not Mauricio A. Petri.

Because of an editing error, an article on Tuesday explaining the purpose of the National Guard referred incorrectly to the police violence against Rodney King that led to riots in Los Angeles in 1992. He was beaten, not killed.

An article on Tuesday about some arts institutions that are distancing themselves from the Ghanaian architect David Adjaye following accusations of sexual misconduct against Adjaye referred incorrectly to the status of a cathedral project in Ghana. The Ghanaian government is canceling the construction contract and ending the use of public funds to pay for it; it is not canceling the project. A final decision on its future awaits an audit.

An article on Wednesday about lessons learned from the 2020 protests and riots in Portland following the murder of George Floyd misstated the given name of the assistant chief with the Portland Police Bureau who was the incident commander for many of the 2020 protests. He is Craig Dobson, not Chris Dobson.

An obituary on Thursday about Joan Kennedy, an accomplished pianist who married into one of America’s foremost political dynasties, misspelled the middle name of Ms. Kennedy’s father. He was Harry Wiggin Bennett Jr., not Wiggins.

An article on Friday about the recipient of the Nobel Prize of Literature, Hungarian novelist Laszlo Krasznahorkai, misidentified which work of his consisted of a single sentence. It is his novel “Herscht 07769,” not “The Melancholy of Resistance.” (This error also appeared in an August 2014 article .)

An article on Wednesday about government disputes over extending expiring health care subsidies, a chart overstated the cost of insurance in the Affordable Care Act marketplace and the value of certain subsidies from 2021 to 2025. The total cost of the premium for 2025 is $8,326, not $9,638.

An article this weekend on Page 6 about the popular Twitch streamer Kai Cenat misstates the pronunciation of Cenat’s surname. It is seh-NAT, not seh-NOT. The article also misidentifies the location of his nonstop livestream last month. It was in Beverly Hills, not the Hollywood Hills neighborhood of Los Angeles.

An obituary on Tuesday about Darleane Hoffman, an innovator in nuclear chemistry, referred incorrectly to two elements on the periodic table of the elements. A transuranium element studied by Albert Ghiorso of the Lawrence Berkeley Lab in 1974 has an atomic number of 106; its atomic weight is not 106. And uranium, a so-called heavy element, has a weight of 238, not 92, which is its atomic number. The obituary also described a transuranium element incorrectly. It refers to any element with an atomic number above 92 on the periodic table of the elements, not an atomic weight above 92.

An obituary on Thursday about Joan Kennedy, the former wife of Senator Edward M. Kennedy, misidentified the editor of Ms. Kennedy’s 1992 book, “The Joy of Classical Music: A Guide for You and Your Family.” It was edited by Nan A. Talese, not Ms. Kennedy’s sister-in-law Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis.
ref_headline: The Joy of Classical Music: A Guide for You and Your Family.

text: An article this weekend on Page 6 about the popular Twitch streamer Kai Cenat misstates the pronunciation of Cenat’s surname. It is seh-NAT, not seh-NOT. The article also misidentifies the location of his nonstop livestream last month. It was in Beverly Hills, not the Hollywood Hills neighborhood of Los Angeles.

An obituary on Sunday about the actress Diane Keaton misstated Nancy Meyers’s role in the movie “Baby Boom,” in which Ms. Keaton starred. She wrote it but did not direct it. A picture caption with a reprint of the obituary on Monday referred incorrectly to Ms. Keaton’s Oscar nomination for her performance in the 2003 comedy “Something’s Gotta Give.” It was her fourth nomination for best actress, not her third. (Her third was for the 1996 drama “Marvin’s Room.” )

Because of an editing error, an article on Sunday about the way a Harvard protest over the Israel-Hamas war has become a touchstone for broader debate over campus unrest misstated the background of a student. He is South Asian, not Southeast Asian.

An article on Sunday about the Sunday routine for Anjula Acharia, who hosts one of the most coveted Diwali balls in New York City every year, misstated the name of Ms. Acharia’s record label. It is 5 Junction, not 5 Junctures.

A picture caption with an obituary on Sunday about John Lodge, the singer and bassist for the British rock group the Moody Blues, misspelled the given name of a member of the band. He was Graeme Edge, not Graham.

Because of an editing error, an article on Tuesday about an N.B.A. player’s adjustment to living in Minnesota and playing for the Timberwolves after they acquired him in a trade with the New York Knicks misspelled his surname in some instances. He is Julius Randle, not Randall.
An article on Tuesday about New York City Ballet’s fashion gala omitted the first name of a dancer with that company. The dancer is Taylor Stanley.

An obituary on Sept. 21 about Jack Daniels, a two-time Olympic medalist in the modern pentathlon and an exercise physiologist, misstated the given name of Daniels’ father. He was Ronald Daniels, not Robert (though he was known informally as Bob).

An article on Wednesday about the naming of Dallin H. Oaks as the next president of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints misstated the age of D. Todd Christofferson, a counselor who will join Mr. Oaks in the First Presidency. He is 80, not 79.

An article on Wednesday about the impact of France’s political turmoil on its economy misstated the title of Olivier Redoulès at Rexecode, a French consultancy. He is an economist there, not the founder.
An obituary on Oct. 9 about Joan Kennedy, the former wife of Senator Edward M. Kennedy, referred incorrectly to one of her survivors. She is survived by a great-grandson, not a great-granddaughter.

A picture caption with an article on Monday about the cease-fire deal in Gaza misstated a central component of the agreement. As the article correctly noted, the deal involved the release of the Israeli hostages in Gaza and the release of Palestinian prisoners and detainees held by Israel; it was not a hostage exchange.

An article on Friday about a growing number of high-ranking Democrats from New York who are urging Gov. Kathy Hochul to reject a plan for an underwater gas pipeline off New York City misstated the deadline by which the Department of Environmental Conservation must decide the fate of the pipeline project. The deadline is the end of November, not next spring.

An article this weekend on Page 16 about current investment and regulatory strategies and their similarities to the speculative excesses that preceded the 1929 crash misstates the requirements for quarterly financial statements filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission. Such statements must be reviewed by an independent accountant; they do not need to be independently audited.
An article this weekend on Page 22 about New York City mayoral candidate Zohran Mamdani misidentifies the office held by Jabari Brisport. He is a state senator, not a state assemblyman.

An article this weekend on Page 14 about the arranging and dismantling of one of the world’s largest and most complete stegosaurus skeletons misstates Melissa Posen’s title at the American Museum of Natural History. She is the vice president of exhibitions, not the senior director of exhibition operations.

An article this weekend on Page 28 about U.S. museums and their recruitment of young people to curate their shows this fall misspells the name of a student curator. It is lk’ayqn Moomaw, not Lkayan Moomaw.
