A news analysis article on Sunday about the lessons from the Richard Nixon era in relation to President Trump’s use of impoundment power misstated the year in which the Supreme Court case Train v. City of New York was decided. The ruling was made in 1975, not 1985.

An article on Monday about the government shutdown’s effects on the operations of the federal court system, starting Oct. 20, referred incorrectly to the fund the judiciary drew on to keep operating after the shutdown. The fund comprised case filing fees and leftover appropriations from previous years, not fees the public pays to gain access to court records online.

An article on Sunday about the actress Glenn Close misstated Close’s familial relationship to C.W. Post. He was the father of Close’s grandfather’s first wife; she is not related by blood to Post. The article referred incorrectly to Close’s living situation in New York in the 1970s. A guy she once dated used a board over the bathtub as a dining table; Close never dined that way in her own apartment. The article also misstated where a neighbor was once seen typing naked in the window; it was at Close’s boyfriend’s apartment, not her own.
An article on Sunday about a handful of artist-led groups using data to explore the climate crisis misstated the name of a London-based artist studio. It is ScanLAB Projects, not ScanLAB.

An article on Sunday about the Portland Art Museum’s $116 million renovation and expansion misstated the name of one of the architectural firms that designed the Mark Rothko Pavilion, the 21,000-square-foot glass structure anchoring the museum. It is Vinci Hamp, not VinciHamp.
An obituary on Monday about the physicist Chen Ning Yang misspelled the name of the German mathematician who developed gauge theory. He was Hermann Weyl, not Herman Wyl.

An article on Friday about the C.I.A.’s involvement in Latin America misstated the year Rafael Leónidas Trujillo Molina, the authoritarian leader of the Dominican Republic, was killed. It was 1961, not 1975.

An article on Saturday about three new cases of monkeypox in California misstated the name of the pharmaceutical company that makes a vaccine for protection against the infection. Jynneos is the name of the vaccine. The manufacturer is Bavarian Nordic.

An article on Monday about the reactions of Jewish New Yorkers after the return of Israeli hostages last week misidentified the member of Leon Goldenberg’s family who was assaulted. The victim was his grandson, not his nephew.
An article on Tuesday about two reported encounters between surfers and sea otters in Santa Cruz, Calif., misstated when the incidents occurred. It was last week, not this week.

A picture caption with an article on Monday about the deportation of a young woman and her mother to Guatemala misidentified the young woman shown dressed for her quinceanera. It was the cousin of Nory Sontay Ramos, not Sontay Ramos.

An article on Monday about Bari Weiss’s first days as the editor of CBS News misstated the air date of a “60 Minutes” episode featuring interviews with Jared Kushner and Steve Witkoff, the architects of President Trump’s Middle East peace plan. It was last Sunday, not this Sunday.
An article on Tuesday about a new musical adaptation of Prince’s 1984 film “Purple Rain” misstated Prince’s involvement with the music club First Avenue. He played there, but he never owned the club.

An article accompanying the obituary about Ace Frehley on Saturday misattributed a quote to Frehley that appeared in a Kiss interview with Rolling Stone. It was Peter Criss who said, “I see myself eventually on my own without the makeup and the bombs, without theatrics. I could dig getting up there with a white suit and three chick singers.”
ref_headline: I see myself eventually on my own without the makeup and the bombs, without theatrics. I could dig getting up there with a white suit and three chick singers.

text: An obituary on Tuesday about the writer Alison Rose misstated where the offices of The New Yorker were located when she began working there as a receptionist. They were on West 43rd Street in Manhattan, not West 45th Street.

An article on Tuesday about political debates over the investment in artificial intelligence in Chile misstated the expected timing of the government’s final plan for data center development in northern Chile. Officials said their hope is to announce the plan by the end of the year, not before national elections next month.

An article on Wednesday about six heist film recommendations incorrectly attributed a fake sculpture of Venus that figured in the movie “How to Steal a Million.” It was the grandfather of Audrey Hepburn’s character, not her father, who created it.

An article on Oct. 15 about the connection between a U.S.-based group that opposes abortion rights and Britain’s ascendant Reform U.K. party referred incorrectly to the 2025 Reform U.K. party conference. It was Nigel Farage’s second conference as leader, not his first.

An article on Tuesday about a study that shows how food allergies in children dropped sharply in the years after new guidelines encouraged parents to introduce infants to peanut products referred imprecisely to the recommendations on the introduction of possible food allergens to infants. Studies recommended introducing a small amount of peanut products, not peanuts, to infants. (Whole peanuts could pose a choking hazard.)

An article on Thursday about President Trump’s acknowledgment on Wednesday that he was having the entire East Wing demolished to make way for a 90,000-square-foot ballroom included a comment from Sara C. Bronin, a law professor at George Washington University, in which she misstated the function of the National Historic Preservation Act. It is not the case that the demolition of the East Wing may run afoul of the act; in fact, the act contains an exception for White House projects.

An article on Thursday about a report’s findings that deforestation happening to expand coffee crops is imperiling the long-term prospects of coffee crops misstated the findings on precipitation in the Amazon. Deforestation has driven about 75 percent of the decrease in rain there, it did not drive a 75 percent drop in rain.

An article on Thursday about the Trump administration’s cuts to cyberdefense as threats have grown misstated the name of the agency whose size had been cut by a third. It is the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, not the Cyberspace and Infrastructure Security Agency.

An article on Friday about King Charles III and Pope Leo XIV praying together in an ecumenical service at the Sistine Chapel on Thursday, which was the first time in hundreds of years that a pontiff and a British monarch have publicly prayed together, misidentified the way in which King Henry VIII chose to dissolve his first of several marriages. The king sought an annulment and not a divorce.

An article on Friday about several measures to pay essential government employees that were blocked in the Senate on Thursday referred incorrectly to the U.S. Postal Service employees’ status in government shutdowns. The Postal Service is separately funded, and its workers continue to receive paychecks; they do not work without pay.

An article this weekend on Page 8 about Vince Gilligan’s new television series, “Pluribus,” included erroneous information from Sony, which misstates the length of Gilligan’s overall production deal with Sony Pictures Television. It is over 15 years, not over 20.
An article this weekend on Page 14 about the director Kathryn Bigelow misstates the number of women who have won the best director Oscar since Bigelow’s historic win. It is two, not one.

An article on Page 13 this weekend about the marketing campaign for Taylor Swift’s album “The Life of a Showgirl” misstates the date when orange doors, an element of the campaign, made appearances in cities around the world. It was Saturday, Oct. 4, not Oct. 5. And the article misstates the title of a movie released as part of the campaign. It was “The Official Release Party of a Showgirl,” not “The Release Party of a Showgirl.”

It was “The Official Release Party of a Showgirl,” not “The Release Party of a Showgirl.”

An article on Monday about the impact that President Trump’s policies have had on Iowans included an outdated figure for the change in Iowa’s economic output in the first quarter of 2025. According to a revised estimate by the Bureau of Economic Analysis, it contracted at a 1.2 percent annual rate in the quarter; the bureau’s initial estimate was a “6.1 percent contraction.”
An article on Sunday about the expectations for President Trump’s nearly weeklong tour of Asia misstated the last time Mr. Trump made an official visit to Southeast Asia. It was 2019, not 2017.

An article on Friday about a marathon speech on the Senate floor by Jeff Merkley, an Oregon Democrat, misstated when the chamber was to vote on legislation to clear payments for some government workers. The vote took place this past Thursday. It is not the case that it was expected this coming Thursday.

Because of an editing error, a picture caption in an article on Friday about the history of the East Wing at the White House misstated a location on the White House grounds in an image from 1962. Jacqueline Kennedy was showing Empress Farah, wife of the Shah of Iran, the White House grounds near the Oval Office, not the East Colonnade.

An article on Saturday about the Justice Department’s case against New York’s attorney general, Letitia James, as an illustration of President Trump’s influence on the department misstated the date on which Ms. James was to appear in a Virginia court. She pleaded not guilty on Friday to one count of bank fraud and one count of making a false statement to a financial institution. It is not the case that she had yet to appear in court.
An obituary on Monday about the actress June Lockhart misstated the name of one of her daughters. She is June Elizabeth Lockhart-Triolo, not June Elizabeth Trola.

Because of an editing error, a news analysis article on Sunday about Speaker Mike Johnson’s decision to put the House on an indefinite hiatus misstated the constitutional powers of the House. It has the sole power to originate tax legislation, not spending legislation.

An article on Tuesday about Nick Mangold, the former All-Pro center for the Jets who died this week, misstated the number of this season’s Super Bowl. The title game, which will be held in February, will be Super Bowl LX, not Super Bowl XL.
A reporter’s notebook article on Tuesday about the nu metal scene misstated the chronology of Korn’s albums. “Follow the Leader” was the band’s third album, not its sophomore album.
An obituary on Oct. 20 about the physicist Chen Ning Yang misstated who survives him. His son Franklin died in 2024; he does not survive him.

Because of an editing error, an article on Thursday about President Trump’s desire to help broker peace between North and South Korea misidentified which gift Mr. Trump joked about wanting to wear. It was a medal, not a crown. The error was repeated in a separate article about South Korea’s agreement on a trade deal with the United States

An article on Monday about the drone manufacturer Fire Point, one of the largest contractors for the Ukrainian military, misstated what action the Public Anticorruption Council called for in response to quality and pricing concerns. The council has considered calling for a parliamentary inquiry but has not yet done so.

An article on Thursday about Radio Free Asia shutting down its news operations for the first time since its founding in 1996 misstated the position of Cameron Lang at the broadcaster. She is the associate general counsel at Radio Free Asia, not the general counsel.

An article on Oct. 26 about the history of the movie theater that was located in the East Wing of the White House misstated the timeline of John F. Kennedy’s viewing of “From Russia With Love” in the theater. He watched it on Oct. 23, 1963, but not a second time on Nov. 21.

An article on Friday about how candy companies are quietly cutting down on cocoa because of climate change included an outdated title for John Satumba. He is now vice president for global food innovation at Cargill, not director of global edible oil solutions research and development.

An article this weekend on Page 4 about the 50th anniversary season of Steppenwolf, one of America’s pre-eminent theater companies, misstated the date “Bug” will begin previews. It is Dec. 16, not Dec. 17.
An article on Sept. 7 about A.I. in art misspelled the surname of a writer and an artist who wrote a book about A.I. and art. His name is Jan Svenungsson, not Svennungsen.
An article on Page 20 this weekend about the comedian Stavros Halkias misstates the title of his half-hour, crowd-work-only special. It is “Four Nights in NYC,” not “Live at the Lodge Room.”

An article on Oct. 19 about the New York City mayoral candidate Zohran Mamdani misidentified the song that bar patrons chanted the candidate’s name to as he left the bar. It was “Seven Nation Army,” not ‘‘Icky Thump.’’

An article on Saturday about fact-checking President Trump’s claim about SNAP funding and partisanship misstated the year of a survey by the Pew Research Center that measured the percentages of Republicans and Democrats who said they had received food stamps. It was in 2012, not 2013.

An article on Sunday about former President Barack Obama calling New York City mayoral candidate Zohran Mamdani on Saturday misstated the number of mayoral candidates endorsed by Mr. Obama since he left office. He has publicly endorsed two other mayoral candidates, not one.

An article on Jan. 22 about the Ghanaian musician Ebo Taylor, using information provided by a publicist, misstated Taylor’s age at the time of publication. He was 89, not 90. The error was repeated in the headline.

An article on Oct. 28 about Saudi Arabia’s artificial intelligence ambitions in the geopolitical tussle for tech power misstated the relationship between Aramco, the Saudi state oil giant, and DeepSeek. Aramco is deploying DeepSeek open-source A.I. technology at one of its data centers. DeepSeek is not using Aramco data centers.

An article on Saturday about the best autumn road trips misstated the century in which Paso Robles, Calif., became a hot springs destination as well as home to a large concentration of almond orchards. It was the 19th century, not the 18th.

An article on Sep. 4 about Kathryn Bigelow’s new thriller, “A House of Dynamite,” misattributed a line in the movie. It was Jared Harris’s character, the secretary of defense, who said, “That’s what $50 billion buys us?,” not Tracy Letts’s character, a general in STRATCOM.

An article on Oct. 28 about Ken Maracle and his handmade wampum belts misstated the year that the “Wampum:Beads of Diplomacy” exhibition was displayed at the McCord Stewart Museum in Montreal. It was 2023, not 2013.

An article on Wednesday about religious organizations struggling to feed New Yorkers during the SNAP stalemate referred imprecisely to SNAP statistics. Of those relying on SNAP for food, 39 percent were children, 20 percent were seniors and 10 percent were people with disabilities. It is not the case that 39 percent of children, 20 percent of seniors and 10 percent of disabled people across the country depend on SNAP.

An article on Wednesday about Vanderbilt University establishing a satellite campus at the General Theological Seminary in New York City referred incorrectly to the Master of Divinity program at the seminary. It is currently among the largest Master of Divinity programs in an Episcopal seminary in the United States. It is not the largest priest training program in the Episcopal Church in the country.

An article on Sunday about how Carbondale, a college town in Illinois’s southern tip, has become a major abortion hub inaccurately described legal access to abortion in Missouri. Access is severely restricted in the state, not banned.

An obituary on Saturday about Cardinal Dominik Duka, a former Archbishop of Prague, referred incorrectly to Cardinal Duka’s offer to resign when he reached 75, the mandatory retirement age for a Catholic bishop. He offered to resign as archbishop , not as a cardinal. (He remained a cardinal at his death.) The obituary also misstated the number of years that Pope Francis kept him on as archbishop after that point. It was four years, not two.

Because of an editing error, an article on Wednesday about this year’s best genre movies misidentified the breed of the dog who stars in “Good Boy.” He is a Nova Scotia duck tolling retriever, not a golden retriever.

An article on Wednesday about Philadelphia’s efforts to deter crime with environmental design referred imprecisely to enhancements made to Philadelphia’s streetlight network. While community efforts helped shape the enhancements as the project proceeded, the streetlight project was already underway.

Because of an editing error, an article on Thursday about Jared Isaacman, the private astronaut and billionaire entrepreneur, being confirmed on Wednesday to lead NASA misspelled the first name of a senator from Washington. She is Maria Cantwell, not Marie.

An article on Thursday about the rarity of lengthy manhunts in high-profile attacks like the recent Brown University shooting has misstated how one of the police officers died after the Boston Marathon bombing. He was wounded by a bomb blast, not gunfire.

An obituary on Friday about the war correspondent Peter Arnett misstated the title of a CNN documentary he anchored in 1998. It was “Valley of Death,” not “Operation Tailwind.” (Operation Tailwind was the name of a U.S. covert military mission in Laos in 1970, the subject of the broadcast.)

Because of an editing error, a picture caption accompanying an article on Sunday about international transplant patients misstated the source of a donation to a foundation. The donation was made by a charity run by the hotel magnate Ryuko Hira, not by Mr. Hira himself.

An obituary on Dec. 16 about the actor and director Rob Reiner referred incorrectly to Kris Perry. She was the second executive director of the California commission charged with overseeing the disbursement of funds from tobacco taxes for early childhood education, now known as First 5 California, not the first. Jane Henderson was the commission’s first executive director.

A news analysis article on Sunday about how President Trump has greatly expanded executive power while embracing the trappings of royalty in ways not seen in the modern era misstated the name of a British royal family residence. It is Windsor Castle, not Windsor Palace.

An article on Tuesday about the Trump administration announcement that it would pause leases for five wind farms under construction off the east coast misspelled the name of the New Bedford mayor. He is Jon Mitchell, not John Mitchell. Because of an editing error, the article also misstated the approval the five wind farms’ received from the Biden administration. They received permits, not leases.

An article on Tuesday about Scott C. Beardsley being appointed president at the University of Virginia misstated the total number of students at the university. It has more than 26,000 students, not more than 40,000.

A critic’s notebook article on Tuesday about two South Korean drama thrillers to stream misidentified an actor in the Hulu series “Nine Puzzles.” The detective character Han-saem was played by Son Seok-koo; Robert M. Lee voiced the character in the English dub.

An obituary on Dec. 16 about the actor and director Rob Reiner misspelled the given name of the actor who played the lead role of Gordie in Mr. Reiner’s film “Stand by Me.” He is Wil Wheaton, not Will.

An article on Monday about how Google has quietly started to roll out a way for users to change their Gmail addresses ending with @gmail.com and keep all their emails, no matter how old their account, misidentified which part of a Gmail account Google has allowed users to change for years. It is the sender name, not the username.

An article on Tuesday about some of the new A.I. billionaires misstated the age of Michael Truell, a co-founder of Cursor. He is 25, not 24. It also misstated the academic status of Mr. Truell and his Cursor co-founders, Sualeh Asif, Aman Sanger and Arvid Lunnemark, at M.I.T. They graduated in 2022; they did not drop out that year.

Because of an editing error, a picture caption with an article on Saturday about memorable images of the year in culture misstated the location of a 100-year-old equestrian statue of Stonewall Jackson. It was in Charlottesville, Va., not Charlottesville, N.C.
